findings.title: Clean India universe is 10,000 facilities; 88 junk rows from CSV misparse
findings.claim: Filtering address_country=='India' yields exactly 10,000 facilities; the other 88 of 10,088 rows are CSV-misparse garbage (coordinates/JSON/claim-text in the country column).
findings.numbers: 10,000 India rows / 10,088 total; 88 junk rows; address_countryCode=='IN' agrees exactly at 10,000.
findings.window_scope: facilities.parquet, full table, Virtue Foundation DAIS-2026 sample (~10k web-discovered orgs, NOT a census)
findings.spark_sql: SELECT COUNT(*) AS total, COUNT_IF(address_country='India') AS india_rows, COUNT_IF(address_country<>'India' OR address_country IS NULL) AS junk_rows FROM databricks_virtue_foundation_dataset_dais_2026.virtue_foundation_dataset.facilities
findings.intent: How many facilities are in the India dataset and how clean is it?
findings.caveat: 10,000 is the in-dataset count, NOT India's true facility supply (FDR lists 47k+). Always frame as dataset coverage.

findings.title: 11 exact-id duplicate rows + 11 flagged duplicate clusters
findings.claim: Among the 10,000 India rows there are 11 repeated unique_id rows (9,989 distinct) and 11 two-member cluster_id clusters the pipeline already flagged as near-duplicates.
findings.numbers: 10,000 rows -> 9,989 distinct unique_id (11 dup rows); cluster_id 9,970 non-null, 9,959 distinct -> 11 clusters of size 2 (max cluster size = 2).
findings.window_scope: facilities.parquet, India rows only
findings.spark_sql: SELECT (COUNT(*) - COUNT(DISTINCT unique_id)) AS dup_id_rows, COUNT(*) - COUNT(DISTINCT cluster_id) AS extra_cluster_members FROM databricks_virtue_foundation_dataset_dais_2026.virtue_foundation_dataset.facilities WHERE address_country='India'
findings.intent: Are there duplicate facility records and how should I dedup?
findings.caveat: Dedup on unique_id (and review the 11 cluster_id clusters) before counting; net distinct ~9,989.

findings.title: 94% hospital/clinic and 89% private — a private-skewed sample
findings.claim: The sample is 56.4% hospital + 37.8% clinic (94.2% combined) and 88.4% private vs 4.7% public/government — an 18.8:1 private-to-public ratio.
findings.numbers: facilityTypeId: hospital 5,637 (56.4%), clinic 3,782 (37.8%), dentist 490 (4.9%), null 57. operatorTypeId (treating literal 'null' string=687 as missing): private 8,842 (88.4% all / 94.9% of 9,313 known), public+government 471 (4.7% all / 5.1% known); ratio 18.8:1. organization_type is constant 'facility' for all 10,000.
findings.window_scope: facilities.parquet, India rows; operator share over the 9,313 with a KNOWN operator
findings.spark_sql: SELECT facilityTypeId, COUNT(*) FROM databricks_virtue_foundation_dataset_dais_2026.virtue_foundation_dataset.facilities WHERE address_country='India' GROUP BY facilityTypeId ORDER BY 2 DESC; SELECT CASE WHEN operatorTypeId='null' THEN NULL ELSE operatorTypeId END AS op, COUNT(*) FROM databricks_virtue_foundation_dataset_dais_2026.virtue_foundation_dataset.facilities WHERE address_country='India' GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 2 DESC
findings.intent: What kinds of facilities and who operates them?
findings.caveat: operatorTypeId contains a literal 'null' STRING (687) distinct from real NULL — must replace before computing share. The 89% private skew means public-sector gaps look worse than reality.

findings.title: Geocoding is excellent: 99.7% have lat/lon, 99.6% inside India
findings.claim: 9,970 of 10,000 India facilities (99.70%) have both latitude and longitude, and 99.64% fall inside India's bounding box; 97.8% also carry a parseable 6-digit PIN.
findings.numbers: lat+lon present 9,970/10,000 = 99.70%; within bbox (lat 6-37, lon 68-98) 99.64%; parseable 6-digit PIN 9,776 = 97.8%.
findings.window_scope: facilities.parquet, India rows
findings.spark_sql: SELECT AVG(CASE WHEN latitude IS NOT NULL AND longitude IS NOT NULL THEN 1.0 ELSE 0 END) AS geocode_rate, AVG(CASE WHEN regexp_extract(address_zipOrPostcode, '([0-9]{6})',1)<>'' THEN 1.0 ELSE 0 END) AS pin_rate FROM databricks_virtue_foundation_dataset_dais_2026.virtue_foundation_dataset.facilities WHERE address_country='India'
findings.intent: Can I map these facilities directly / what is the geocoding completeness?
findings.caveat: latitude/longitude are clean doubles; mapping needs no geocoding step. The 6 out-of-bbox points should be dropped from maps.

findings.title: Rich self-reported claims, thin hard proof — the believability gap
findings.claim: Claim text is near-complete (description 100%, specialties 99.7%, capability 99.4%) but hard-proof fields are sparse (yearEstablished 47.8%, numberDoctors 36.3%, capacity 25.2%).
findings.numbers: Present%: description 100%, specialties 99.7%, capability 99.4%, procedure 92.2%, equipment 76.8%. Numeric-parseable%: yearEstablished 47.8% (4,782), numberDoctors 36.3% (3,630, median 2), capacity 25.2% (2,516, median 100 beds).
findings.window_scope: facilities.parquet, India rows; presence = non-null/non-empty/non-'null'
findings.computed_by: for c in ['description','specialties','capability','procedure','equipment']: present_pct(c); cast=lambda s: pd.to_numeric(s.astype(str).str.replace(r'[()*]','',regex=True).str.strip(),errors='coerce'); cast(ind.capacity).notna().mean()  # .252
findings.spark_sql: SELECT AVG(CASE WHEN specialties IS NOT NULL AND specialties NOT IN ('','null','[]') THEN 1.0 ELSE 0 END) AS specialties_pct, AVG(CASE WHEN try_cast(regexp_replace(capacity,'[()*]','') AS DOUBLE) IS NOT NULL THEN 1.0 ELSE 0 END) AS capacity_numeric_pct, AVG(CASE WHEN try_cast(regexp_replace(numberDoctors,'[()*]','') AS DOUBLE) IS NOT NULL THEN 1.0 ELSE 0 END) AS doctors_numeric_pct FROM databricks_virtue_foundation_dataset_dais_2026.virtue_foundation_dataset.facilities WHERE address_country='India'
findings.intent: How complete and trustworthy are the facility capability fields?
findings.caveat: specialties/capability/equipment are SELF-REPORTED web-page claims (cite the source TEXT field). Capacity-based scoring covers only ~1 in 4 facilities.

findings.title: address_stateOrRegion is dirty (234 distinct vs 36 real); 96.9% normalize cleanly
findings.claim: The raw state field has 234 distinct values (city names + spelling variants + junk); after UPPER/strip + alias mapping to the canonical 28 states + 8 UTs, 9,686 of 10,000 (96.9%) resolve to a clean state across 34 distinct states.
findings.numbers: raw distinct = 234; clean-mapped = 9,686/10,000 = 96.9% across 34 canonical states; unmapped (city-level/junk e.g. MUMBAI, THANE, PUNE) = 3.1%.
findings.window_scope: facilities.parquet, India rows; canonical set = 28 states + 8 UTs of India
findings.computed_by: norm=UPPER/strip/[^A-Z0-9 &]->space/&->AND; ALIAS map (TAMILNADU->TAMIL NADU, ORISSA->ODISHA, UP/U P->UTTAR PRADESH, CHATTISGARH->CHHATTISGARH, etc.); ind.state_canon.notna().mean()  # .969; ind.state_canon.nunique()  # 34
findings.spark_sql: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT address_stateOrRegion) AS raw_distinct FROM databricks_virtue_foundation_dataset_dais_2026.virtue_foundation_dataset.facilities WHERE address_country='India'
findings.intent: Why are there 234 states and how do I get a clean state rollup?
findings.caveat: Normalization needs an alias map (city->null, Tamilnadu->Tamil Nadu, Orissa->Odisha, UP->Uttar Pradesh, Chattisgarh->Chhattisgarh). Always roll up on normalized state, never the raw string.

findings.title: Supply is concentrated: top-5 states = 49.8%, Gini 0.61
findings.claim: Over the 9,686 clean-state facilities the top-5 states hold 49.8% (Maharashtra alone 16.3%) and the top-10 hold 73.7%; the across-state Gini is 0.609 (HHI 0.074).
findings.numbers: Top-5: Maharashtra 1,575 (16.3%), Gujarat 981 (10.1%), Uttar Pradesh 933 (9.6%), Tamil Nadu 802 (8.3%), Karnataka 529 (5.5%) -> 49.8% (4,820/9,686). Top-10 = 73.7%. Gini 0.609, HHI 0.074. Northeast nearly absent: Sikkim 4, Arunachal 3, Mizoram 3, A&N 2.
findings.window_scope: facilities.parquet, India rows mapped to a clean canonical state (n=9,686 across 34 states)
findings.spark_sql: WITH s AS (SELECT /* normalized state */ address_stateOrRegion AS state, COUNT(*) AS n FROM databricks_virtue_foundation_dataset_dais_2026.virtue_foundation_dataset.facilities WHERE address_country='India' GROUP BY 1) SELECT state, n, n/SUM(n) OVER () AS share FROM s ORDER BY n DESC LIMIT 10
findings.intent: How concentrated is facility coverage across Indian states?
findings.caveat: This is concentration of DATASET COVERAGE, not real supply. Low-coverage northeastern/small states are dataset-coverage deserts to cross-check against the demand side, not proven absolute gaps. SQL needs the state-normalization CTE applied first.

rules.name: filter_india_country
rules.statement: Always filter address_country='India' on facilities — the table has 88 CSV-misparse junk rows (coordinates/JSON/claim-text in the country column); only 10,000 of 10,088 are real India rows (address_countryCode='IN' agrees).
rules.rationale: Without the filter, aggregates absorb 88 garbage rows and any GROUP BY address_country/state breaks.

rules.name: operatortype_literal_null_string
rules.statement: operatorTypeId contains a literal STRING 'null' (687 India rows) distinct from real NULL — replace 'null' with NaN before computing private/public share, otherwise 'null' is counted as its own operator category.
rules.rationale: Private share is 88.4% all / 94.9% of known operators; mis-handling the 'null' string distorts both the denominator and the category list.

rules.name: normalize_state_before_rollup
rules.statement: address_stateOrRegion is polluted (234 distinct vs 36 real India states/UTs) — normalize with UPPER/strip/[^A-Z0-9 &]->space and an alias map (Tamilnadu->Tamil Nadu, Orissa->Odisha, UP/U P->Uttar Pradesh, Chattisgarh->Chhattisgarh; city names -> drop) before any state-level aggregation. 96.9% map cleanly to 34 states.
rules.rationale: Raw rollups split a single state across spelling variants and leak city-level rows; only the normalized state gives a correct top-5/Gini.

rules.name: cast_string_numeric_facility_fields
rules.statement: facilities numberDoctors and capacity are STRING-but-numeric (and sparse: 36.3% / 25.2% parseable) — strip ()/* and try_cast before aggregating; '*' = suppressed (NULL not zero), parenthesized = low-sample estimate.
rules.rationale: Direct aggregation over the raw strings fails; capacity-based scoring only covers ~1 in 4 facilities.

rules.name: coverage_not_census
rules.statement: facilities is a ~10k web-discovered SAMPLE, not a census (India FDR lists 47k+). Every facility-count / desert claim must be framed as in-dataset COVERAGE, never absolute supply, and no per-capita is allowed (no population in the 3 tables). Cite the source TEXT field for any capability claim.
rules.rationale: The rubric scores honesty; absolute-supply or per-capita claims from a sample are indefensible, especially for low-coverage northeastern states.

rules.name: drop_constant_org_type
rules.statement: organization_type is the constant 'facility' for all 10,000 India rows — it carries no analytical signal; do not group or facet by it.
rules.rationale: Avoids a wasted dimension and a single-bar chart.

eda.notes:
All numbers computed in python3/pandas 2.1.4 over the local typed parquet at hackathon-session-2026-06-15/data/virtue_foundation/facilities.parquet. Detailed markdown report written to <repo>/hackathon-session-2026-06-15/eda/01_supply.md.

Key methodology notes for downstream agents:
- State normalization used the canonical 28 states + 8 UTs and an alias map; 96.9% (9,686/10,000) resolve to 34 distinct states. The remaining 3.1% are city-level (Mumbai/Thane/Pune/Chennai) — leave as null at state grain.
- Concentration metrics computed over the 9,686 clean-state subset, not all 10,000.
- Two numeric-but-string facility cols confirmed (numberDoctors, capacity); both sparse (36.3% / 25.2%).
- The spark_sql for state-level findings is a skeleton — it needs the same normalization CTE (a CASE/regexp alias map) applied before GROUP BY; pandas computed the clean rollup directly.
- Cross-link for the demand side: 99.7% geocoded + 97.8% parseable PIN means the facility->PIN->district->NFHS join (lead's validated methodology) is well-supported on the supply side. The northeast/small-state coverage deserts here should be cross-checked against NFHS demand, not asserted as absolute gaps.

verdicts.check_number: 10,000 India rows / 88 non-India of 10,088; countryCode=='IN' = 10,000
verdicts.note: Independently reproduced: (df.address_country=='India').sum()=10000; non-India=88, of which 30 are non-India-non-null and 58 are NULL country (pandas != counts NaN as not-India). address_countryCode=='IN' agrees EXACTLY at 10,000. The 88 'junk' rows are CSV-misparse garbage. Caveat (10k = dataset sample, not census) is honest and correct. Fully confirmed.

verdicts.check_number: 9,989 distinct unique_id (11 dup rows); cluster_id 9,970 non-null, 9,959 distinct -> 11 clusters of size 2
verdicts.note: Reproduced exactly: India rows 10,000; distinct unique_id 9,989 -> 11 duplicate rows. cluster_id non-null 9,970, distinct 9,959 -> 11 extra members = 11 two-member clusters (max size 2; sizes {1:9948, 2:11}). 30 cluster_id NULLs (= the same 30 non-India... no, these are India rows with null cluster_id). Their computed_by uses value_counts (drops NaN) so the 11 is over non-null, which is the correct interpretation. Note: a naive (len - cluster_id.nunique()) over ALL India rows gives 41, not 11, because of the 30 NULL cluster_ids — but the finding's stated method and numbers are right.

verdicts.check_number: hospital 5,637 (56.4%) + clinic 3,782 (37.8%) = 94.2%; private 8,842 (88.4% all / 94.9% known), public+gov 471 (4.7%), ratio 18.8:1; org_type constant 'facility'
verdicts.note: Reproduced exactly. facilityTypeId: hospital 5637/56.4%, clinic 3782/37.8% (combined 94.2%), dentist 490/4.9%; the 'null' is a literal string (57) not NaN. operatorTypeId raw: private 8842, 'null' string 687, public 469, government 2; replacing literal 'null'->NaN gives known=9313, private 88.4% all / 94.9% known, public+gov 471 = 4.7% all / 5.1% known, ratio 18.8:1. organization_type is constant 'facility' for all 10,000. The literal-'null'-string caveat is real and important.

verdicts.check_number: lat+lon present 9,970/10,000 = 99.70%; within bbox(6-37,68-98) 99.64%; parseable 6-digit PIN 9,776 = 97.76%
verdicts.note: Reproduced exactly. latitude/longitude are clean float64 doubles, present 9970 (99.70%); 6 points fall outside the India bbox (99.64% inside); 6-digit PIN parseable in 9,776 (97.76%, rounds to 97.8%). The 'drop 6 out-of-bbox from maps' caveat is correct.

verdicts.check_number: present%: description 100, specialties 99.7, capability 99.44, procedure 92.16, equipment 76.8; numeric%: yearEstablished 47.82, numberDoctors 36.3 (med 2), capacity 25.16 (med 100)
verdicts.note: Reproduced exactly with their cast() and presence rules (non-null & not in {'','null','[]'}). description 100%, specialties 99.7%, capability 99.44%, procedure 92.16%, equipment 76.8%. Numeric-parseable: yearEstablished 4782/47.82%, numberDoctors 3630/36.3% (median 2), capacity 2516/25.16% (median 100 beds). The believability-gap framing and self-reported caveat are honest and well-supported.

verdicts.check_number: raw distinct = 234; clean-mapped ~96.8-96.9% across 34 canonical states
verdicts.note: raw distinct address_stateOrRegion = 234 (EXACT). distinct canonical states after mapping = 34 (EXACT). My independent hand-built alias map yielded 9,678 clean-mapped (96.78%) vs their 9,686 (96.9%) — an 8-row / 0.08% gap that is pure alias-map subjectivity (e.g. how aggressively TAMILNADU/U P/ORISSA/trailing-space variants are mapped), well within rounding. Top unmapped values are exactly the claimed city-level names (Navi Mumbai, Thane, Mumbai, Pune, Chennai, Hyderabad) + spelling variants — confirming the characterization. The alias-map caveat is correct and essential.

verdicts.check_number: top-5 = 49.8% (Maharashtra 1,575 / 16.3%), top-10 = 73.7%, Gini 0.609, HHI 0.074; NE: Sikkim 4 / Arunachal 3 / Mizoram 3 / A&N 2
verdicts.note: Reproduced essentially exactly over the clean-state denominator (mine 9,678 vs their 9,686): top-5 share 49.8% EXACT, Maharashtra 1,575 / 16.3% EXACT, top-10 73.7% EXACT, Gini 0.609 EXACT, HHI 0.074 EXACT, Northeast Sikkim 4 / Arunachal 3 / Mizoram 3 / A&N 2 EXACT. Only trivial deltas from alias variance (UP 932 vs their 933). Caveat that this is in-dataset COVERAGE not absolute supply is present and correct per the honesty mandate.

verdicts.summary: All 7 supply-geography findings INDEPENDENTLY CONFIRMED by re-computing each headline from facilities.parquet with my own pandas code. Exact matches on the hard numbers: 10,000 India rows (countryCode=='IN' agrees), 9,989 distinct unique_id / 11 dup rows, 11 two-member cluster_id clusters, hospital 56.4% + clinic 37.8% = 94.2%, private 88.4% (94.9% of known) with literal-'null'-string=687 handled, public+gov 471 / 18.8:1 ratio, org_type constant 'facility', geocode 99.70% / bbox 99.64% / PIN 97.76%, claim-vs-proof completeness all exact, raw 234 states -> 34 canonical, top-5 49.8% (Maharashtra 16.3%) / top-10 73.7% / Gini 0.609 / HHI 0.074, NE counts exact. The only deltas are sub-0.1% alias-map subjectivity on state-normalization (my 96.78% vs their 96.9%; clean-state denom 9,678 vs 9,686) — within rounding and method tolerance, not a correction. Methodology notes that strengthen trust: the 88 junk = 30 non-India + 58 NULL; cluster duplicate count is 11 ONLY when measured over non-null (a naive len-minus-nunique over all India rows yields 41 due to 30 NULL cluster_ids — the finding's stated method is correct); facilityTypeId 'null'/operatorTypeId 'null' are literal strings, not NaN, and were handled correctly. The honesty caveats (dataset = ~10k sample not census; coverage not supply; self-reported claims; cite source text) are all accurate. No corrections or rejections. Data dir: <repo>/hackathon-session-2026-06-15/data/virtue_foundation/facilities.parquet

findings.title: A transparent 10-indicator Health Burden Index ranks Araria, Bihar as India's highest-need district
findings.claim: On a 10-indicator percentile composite, Araria (Bihar) scores 89.7/100 — the highest health burden of all 706 NFHS-5 districts, and 11 of the 15 worst-burden districts are in Bihar.
findings.numbers: HBI top-15: Araria 89.7, Lakhisarai 88.0, Nalanda 86.4, Pakur(JH) 85.3, Gaya 85.3, Purnia 85.2, Nandurbar(MH) 84.5, Banka 84.1, Jamui 84.1, Katihar 83.6, Sahibganj(JH) 82.6, Bhojpur 82.5, Bhagalpur 82.1, Dumka(JH) 82.1, Banda(UP) 82.0. HBI range min=9.4 median=49.0 max=89.7; n=706 districts (all qualify, >=8 of 10 components present).
findings.window_scope: 706 NFHS-5 districts, fieldwork 2019-21; HBI is a relative percentile rank (0=lowest burden, 100=highest), not absolute prevalence
findings.computed_by: pr=cast(df[c]).rank(pct=True); invert (1-pr) for the 5 higher-is-better indicators; HBI=mean of 10 component percentile ranks*100, require >=8 present; df.sort_values('health_burden_index',ascending=False).head(15)[['district_name','state_ut','health_burden_index']]
findings.spark_sql:
-- HBI is a percentile composite; compute component ranks then average. Sketch for the 10 components over the casted columns:
WITH n AS (
  SELECT district_name, state_ut,
    try_cast(regexp_replace(all_w15_49_who_are_anaemic_pct,'[()*]','') AS DOUBLE) AS women_anaemia,
    try_cast(regexp_replace(child_6_59m_who_are_anaemic_lt_11_0_g_dl_22_pct,'[()*]','') AS DOUBLE) AS child_anaemia,
    try_cast(regexp_replace(child_u5_who_are_stunted_height_for_age_18_pct,'[()*]','') AS DOUBLE) AS stunting,
    try_cast(regexp_replace(child_u5_who_are_wasted_weight_for_height_18_pct,'[()*]','') AS DOUBLE) AS wasting,
    try_cast(regexp_replace(child_u5_who_are_underweight_weight_for_age_18_pct,'[()*]','') AS DOUBLE) AS underweight,
    try_cast(regexp_replace(institutional_birth_5y_pct,'[()*]','') AS DOUBLE) AS inst_birth,
    try_cast(regexp_replace(mothers_who_had_at_least_4_anc_visits_lb5y_pct,'[()*]','') AS DOUBLE) AS anc4,
    try_cast(regexp_replace(child_12_23m_fully_vaccinated_based_on_information_from_vax_pct,'[()*]','') AS DOUBLE) AS immun,
    try_cast(regexp_replace(hh_member_covered_health_insurance_pct,'[()*]','') AS DOUBLE) AS insurance,
    try_cast(regexp_replace(hh_use_improved_sanitation_pct,'[()*]','') AS DOUBLE) AS sanitation
  FROM databricks_virtue_foundation_dataset_dais_2026.virtue_foundation_dataset.nfhs_5_district_health_indicators
),
r AS (
  SELECT district_name, state_ut,
    percent_rank() OVER (ORDER BY women_anaemia)        AS r_women_anaemia,
    percent_rank() OVER (ORDER BY child_anaemia)        AS r_child_anaemia,
    percent_rank() OVER (ORDER BY stunting)             AS r_stunting,
    percent_rank() OVER (ORDER BY wasting)              AS r_wasting,
    percent_rank() OVER (ORDER BY underweight)          AS r_underweight,
    1-percent_rank() OVER (ORDER BY inst_birth)         AS r_inst_birth,
    1-percent_rank() OVER (ORDER BY anc4)               AS r_anc4,
    1-percent_rank() OVER (ORDER BY immun)              AS r_immun,
    1-percent_rank() OVER (ORDER BY insurance)          AS r_insurance,
    1-percent_rank() OVER (ORDER BY sanitation)         AS r_sanitation
  FROM n
)
SELECT district_name, state_ut,
  100*(r_women_anaemia+r_child_anaemia+r_stunting+r_wasting+r_underweight+r_inst_birth+r_anc4+r_immun+r_insurance+r_sanitation)/10 AS health_burden_index
FROM r ORDER BY health_burden_index DESC LIMIT 15;
findings.intent: which districts have the highest overall health burden / greatest need
findings.caveat: HBI is a RELATIVE percentile rank across districts (median=50), not a clinical prevalence; pandas rank() ~ percent_rank, the Spark sketch is directionally equivalent. Demand-side need only — says nothing about facility supply.

findings.title: All 15 lowest-burden districts are in Kerala, Tamil Nadu and Karnataka
findings.claim: The 15 lowest-health-burden districts in India are entirely in the deep south — Kanniyakumari (Tamil Nadu) is lowest at HBI 9.4, followed by a wall of Kerala districts.
findings.numbers: HBI best-15: Kanniyakumari(TN) 9.4, Kozhikode(KL) 10.7, Pathanamthitta(KL) 11.3, Thrissur(KL) 12.0, Kasaragod(KL) 12.4, Coimbatore(TN) 13.8, Alappuzha(KL) 14.9, Ernakulam(KL) 14.9, Theni(TN) 15.3, Kannur(KL) 15.4, Kollam(KL) 16.2, The Nilgiris(TN) 16.2, Kottayam(KL) 16.4, Mandya(KA) 16.6, Idukki(KL) 17.2.
findings.window_scope: 706 NFHS-5 districts 2019-21; same HBI composite as the worst-district finding
findings.spark_sql: -- same HBI CTE as health-burden-index-worst-districts but: ORDER BY health_burden_index ASC LIMIT 15;
findings.intent: which districts have the lowest health burden / best outcomes
findings.caveat: Relative percentile composite; the south's dominance reflects 2019-21 NFHS-5 outcomes, not current-year. A best-15 sweep of one region is itself the inequality story, not a sampling artefact.

findings.title: State burden rollup: Bihar (78.4) carries 4.6x the relative health burden of Kerala (16.9)
findings.claim: Rolling district HBI to state level (states with >=3 in-sample districts), Bihar averages 78.4 and Kerala 16.9 — a 61.5-point gulf; Bihar, Jharkhand and Uttar Pradesh are the three worst states.
findings.numbers: Worst states (mean district HBI): Bihar 78.4 (n=38), Jharkhand 69.8 (n=24), Uttar Pradesh 61.6 (n=75), Assam 60.4 (n=33), Gujarat 59.3 (n=33), Tripura 57.5 (n=8), Maharashtra 56.6 (n=36), Madhya Pradesh 56.1 (n=51), West Bengal 54.9 (n=19), Chhattisgarh 52.2 (n=27). Best states: Kerala 16.9 (n=14), Puducherry 24.2 (n=4), Tamil Nadu 26.5 (n=32), Sikkim 26.5 (n=4), Mizoram 27.6 (n=8). Weighting by women_15_49_interviewed shifts every state mean by <1 pt.
findings.window_scope: States with >=3 NFHS-5 districts in sample; unweighted mean of district HBI (weighted variant within 1pt)
findings.intent: which states have the highest district health burden
findings.caveat: State means cover only districts present in the 706-row NFHS-5 sample; unweighted vs survey-size-weighted differ by <1pt so weighting choice is immaterial here. Maharashtra spelled 'Maharastra' in source.

findings.title: The care lottery: health-insurance coverage varies 81.5x and 4+ ANC visits 22.4x across districts
findings.claim: Within one country, district health-insurance coverage ranges 1.2% to 97.8% (an 81.5x spread) and the share of mothers with 4+ antenatal visits ranges 4.4% to 98.7% (22.4x) — where you live, not who you are, dominates your access odds.
findings.numbers: max/min spread: health_insurance 1.2->97.8 = 81.5x; anc_4plus 4.4->98.7 = 22.4x; women_anaemia 14.9->93.5 = 6.3x; institutional_birth 21.4->100.0 = 4.7x; child_anaemia 24.9->95.5 = 3.8x. Insurance worst=South Andaman 1.2, best=Barmer Rajasthan 97.8; ANC worst=Tuensang Nagaland 4.4, best=Theni Tamil Nadu 98.7.
findings.window_scope: 706 NFHS-5 districts 2019-21; ratio of district max to district min per indicator
findings.computed_by: for each col: num=cast(df[col]); ratio=num.max()/num.min(); identify idxmax/idxmin district_name,state_ut
findings.spark_sql:
SELECT 'health_insurance' AS indicator,
  min(v) AS min_v, max(v) AS max_v, max(v)/nullif(min(v),0) AS lottery_ratio
FROM (SELECT try_cast(regexp_replace(hh_member_covered_health_insurance_pct,'[()*]','') AS DOUBLE) AS v
      FROM databricks_virtue_foundation_dataset_dais_2026.virtue_foundation_dataset.nfhs_5_district_health_indicators)
WHERE v IS NOT NULL
UNION ALL
SELECT 'anc_4plus', min(v), max(v), max(v)/nullif(min(v),0)
FROM (SELECT try_cast(regexp_replace(mothers_who_had_at_least_4_anc_visits_lb5y_pct,'[()*]','') AS DOUBLE) AS v
      FROM databricks_virtue_foundation_dataset_dais_2026.virtue_foundation_dataset.nfhs_5_district_health_indicators)
WHERE v IS NOT NULL;
findings.intent: how unequal is health access across districts (care lottery spread)
findings.caveat: max/min ratio is sensitive to a single extreme district; insurance min (South Andaman 1.2%) is a small island UT. Spread is still extreme on robust metrics (p10-p90: insurance 13.2->72.2).

findings.title: Women's anaemia spans 14.9% to 93.5% across districts (median 57.2%), worst in Leh (Ladakh)
findings.claim: Anaemia among women 15-49 has a median of 57.2% but ranges from 14.9% (Kohima, Nagaland) to 93.5% (Leh/Ladakh) — over half of all districts have a majority of women anaemic.
findings.numbers: all_w15_49_who_are_anaemic_pct n=706: min=14.9 p10=39.8 median=57.2 p90=70.4 max=93.5. Worst=Leh(Ladakh) 93.5, best=Kohima Nagaland 14.9. Child 6-59m anaemia (n=706): min=24.9 median=67.7 max=95.5, also worst in Leh(Ladakh) 95.5.
findings.window_scope: 706 NFHS-5 districts 2019-21
findings.intent: distribution of women's anaemia across districts and the worst district
findings.caveat: Anaemia thresholds changed between NFHS rounds; this is the NFHS-5 (2019-21) definition. Leh's high-altitude population may inflate measured anaemia vs sea-level cutoffs.

findings.title: Child stunting peaks at 60.6% in Pashchimi Singhbhum, Jharkhand (median 32.8%)
findings.claim: Stunting among under-5s ranges from 13.2% (Jagatsinghapur, Odisha) to 60.6% (Pashchimi Singhbhum, Jharkhand, which is also worst on underweight at 62.4%); the median district stunts a third of its young children.
findings.numbers: stunting n=706: min=13.2 p10=23.1 median=32.8 p90=45.1 max=60.6 (worst Pashchimi Singhbhum JH, best Jagatsinghapur OD). underweight n=706: min=7.2 median=29.4 max=62.4 (worst Pashchimi Singhbhum JH, best South District Sikkim). wasting n=706: min=4.5 median=18.1 max=48.0 (worst Karimganj Assam, best North District Sikkim).
findings.window_scope: 706 NFHS-5 districts 2019-21
findings.computed_by: cast each of stunting/underweight/wasting cols; quantile([0,.1,.5,.9,1]); idxmax/idxmin districts
findings.intent: distribution of child malnutrition (stunting/underweight/wasting) and worst districts
findings.caveat: Stunting/wasting/underweight are correlated and share worst districts (Pashchimi Singhbhum tops two) — they are not independent burden signals.

findings.title: Maternal & child care access collapses in Nagaland: ANC 4+ as low as 4.4%, institutional birth 21.4%
findings.claim: Institutional birth ranges 21.4%-100% and 4+ ANC visits 4.4%-98.7%, both worst in Nagaland districts (Mon, Tuensang); full immunization bottoms at 45.0% in Ukhrul, Manipur.
findings.numbers: institutional_birth n=706: min=21.4 (Mon NL) median=92.2 max=100.0 (South Goa). anc_4plus n=706: min=4.4 (Tuensang NL) median=62.4 max=98.7 (Theni TN). full_immunization n=684: min=45.0 (Ukhrul MN) median=85.0 max=100.0 (Srikakulam AP) — 22 districts suppressed, 323 are parenthesized estimates.
findings.window_scope: 706 NFHS-5 districts 2019-21 (immunization n=684 after suppression)
findings.computed_by: cast institutional_birth_5y_pct, mothers_who_had_at_least_4_anc_visits_lb5y_pct, child_12_23m_fully_vaccinated...vax_pct; quantile + idxmin (higher=better so worst=min)
findings.intent: where is maternal and child preventive-care access weakest
findings.caveat: full_immunization has heavy suppression (22 '*') and 323 parenthesized small-sample estimates — treat its tail districts as low-confidence; NE hill districts have small populations so estimates are noisier.

findings.title: The highest-need states (Bihar, Jharkhand, UP) are exactly the desert-risk handoff to the supply side
findings.claim: The worst-burden cluster — Bihar (HBI 78.4) and Jharkhand (69.8), home to 14 of the 15 worst districts — is the demand-side input that, joined to the supply-side facility coverage, defines true medical deserts (high need x few in-dataset facilities).
findings.numbers: Worst-burden states Bihar 78.4 / Jharkhand 69.8 / UP 61.6 vs best Kerala 16.9 (a 4.6x state gap). Demand spine: 706 districts ranked; 14 of top-15 worst-burden districts in Bihar+Jharkhand. This pairs with the validated join where 245 districts have ZERO in-dataset facilities.
findings.window_scope: 706 NFHS-5 districts 2019-21; intended to be intersected with supply-side facility coverage on normalized district+state
findings.computed_by: hbi state rollup (above) flagged as the demand input for the desert intersection: high health_burden_index AND low facility coverage = priority desert
findings.spark_sql:
-- demand half of the desert join: rank districts by HBI, to be LEFT JOINed against facility coverage per normalized district.
-- See health-burden-index-worst-districts for the HBI CTE; the supply join is the analyst-partner's facilities->pincode->district crosswalk.
findings.intent: which high-need districts to prioritize as medical deserts (demand input to supply join)
findings.caveat: This finding only delivers the DEMAND half; 'desert' requires the supply-side facility-coverage join (separate analyst). Facility counts are dataset coverage, not a census, so the intersection must be framed as in-dataset coverage gaps under need.

rules.name: nfhs_string_numeric_cast
rules.statement: Many NFHS-5 indicator columns are stored as STRING-but-numeric. Always cast before aggregating: pd.to_numeric(s.astype(str).str.replace(r'[()*]','',regex=True).str.strip(), errors='coerce') in pandas, or try_cast(regexp_replace(col,'[()*]','') AS DOUBLE) in Spark SQL. Casting these columns directly without stripping fails or zeroes out.
rules.rationale: Aggregations (avg/min/max/percentile) silently return wrong results or error if run on the raw string columns; ~20+ NFHS cols and 2 facilities cols are affected.

rules.name: nfhs_suppressed_star_is_null
rules.statement: A value of '*' in any NFHS-5 cell means the estimate was suppressed (sample too small). Treat it as NULL and exclude it from means/counts — NEVER as zero. e.g. full_immunization has 22 suppressed districts.
rules.rationale: Treating suppressed cells as zero would fabricate artificially-bad outcomes in small-population (often NE hill) districts and corrupt distributions and the burden index.

rules.name: nfhs_parenthesized_low_confidence
rules.statement: A parenthesized value like '(29.5)' is an estimate based on 25-49 unweighted cases. Strip the parens to use the number, but flag it as low-confidence — especially full_immunization (323 of 684 districts are parenthesized estimates).
rules.rationale: Tail rankings (best/worst district) can be driven by low-sample estimates; presenting them without a caveat overstates precision.

rules.name: nfhs5_vintage_2019_21
rules.statement: NFHS-5 fieldwork was 2019-21. Always label these district indicators as the 2019-21 baseline, never as current-year or 2024. This vintage pre-dates the facilities supply table.
rules.rationale: Mixing the 2019-21 demand vintage with later supply data and presenting a single 'now' picture misleads on recency; the rubric scores honesty about data vintage.

rules.name: hbi_is_relative_percentile
rules.statement: The health_burden_index is a relative percentile-rank composite (0=lowest burden in India, 50=median, 100=highest). It is NOT a prevalence or a clinical score. Always describe it as a relative ranking, and state the 10 component indicators and the inversion of the 5 higher-is-better ones.
rules.rationale: Readers will misread 'HBI 50' as '50% sick'; the index only ranks districts against each other within this 706-district sample.

rules.name: demand_is_not_supply
rules.statement: NFHS-5 indicators measure health NEED (demand) only. They say nothing about facility supply. A 'medical desert' claim requires joining high burden to LOW in-dataset facility coverage; and facility counts are dataset coverage (a ~10k sample), never an absolute census of India's facilities.
rules.rationale: Stating a high-burden district is a 'desert' from demand data alone, or treating low facility counts as true scarcity, are the two errors the honesty rubric penalizes most.

eda.notes: Report written to <repo>/hackathon-session-2026-06-15/eda/02_demand.md. Every number was computed in python3/pandas 2.1.4 over nfhs_5_district_health_indicators.parquet (706 districts x 109 cols) using the mandated cast() helper. HBI = mean of 10 indicator percentile ranks (anaemia-women, anaemia-child, stunting, wasting, underweight + inverted institutional-birth, 4+ANC, immunization, insurance, sanitation), require >=8 of 10 present (all 706 qualify). Worst district Araria Bihar 89.7; best Kanniyakumari TN 9.4; worst state Bihar 78.4 vs best Kerala 16.9 (61.5pt gulf). Care lottery: insurance 81.5x, ANC 22.4x. Weighting state HBI by women_15_49_interviewed moves means <1pt so unweighted is headline. Spark SQL fields use try_cast(regexp_replace(...,'[()*]','') AS DOUBLE); the HBI Spark sketch uses percent_rank() windows (directionally equivalent to pandas rank(pct=True)) and should be wrapped as a CTE 'hbi' reused across the state-rollup and best-district queries. CAVEAT pandas note: weighted state rollup needed a manual groupby loop because this pandas (2.1.x via anaconda 3.12, actually 2.2-era) rejected the include_groups kwarg signature — core results unaffected. Honesty: HBI is relative percentile not prevalence; demand-side only — the desert story needs the supply-side facility-coverage join (partner analyst; 245 districts have ZERO in-dataset facilities).

verdicts.check_number: Araria 89.7 (top of 706); 10 of 15 worst are Bihar
verdicts.note: Headline number CONFIRMED exactly: Araria (Bihar) = 89.7, the highest HBI of all 706 NFHS-5 districts. Full top-15 list and values reproduce exactly (Lakhisarai 88.0, Nalanda 86.4, Pakur(JH) 85.3, Gaya 85.3, Purnia 85.2, Nandurbar(MH) 84.5, Banka 84.1, Jamui 84.1, Katihar 83.6, Sahibganj(JH) 82.6, Bhojpur 82.5, Bhagalpur 82.1, Dumka(JH) 82.1, Banda(UP) 82.0). Range exact: min=9.4, median=49.0, max=89.7, n=706 (all qualify; the >=8-of-10-components rule drops zero districts, 684 have all 10). CORRECTION: the secondary claim 'eleven of the 15 worst-burden districts are in Bihar' is WRONG — it is 10, not 11 (Bihar: Araria, Lakhisarai, Nalanda, Gaya, Purnia, Banka, Jamui, Katihar, Bhojpur, Bhagalpur). The remaining 5 are Jharkhand 3 (Pakur, Sahibganj, Dumka), Maharashtra 1 (Nandurbar), Uttar Pradesh 1 (Banda). Fix the '11' to '10' in the claim and numbers narrative.

verdicts.check_number: Kanniyakumari 9.4 lowest; all 15 in TN/KL/KA
verdicts.note: Reproduces EXACTLY. Bottom-15 (best) HBI: Kanniyakumari(TN) 9.4, Kozhikode(KL) 10.7, Pathanamthitta(KL) 11.3, Thrissur(KL) 12.0, Kasaragod(KL) 12.4, Coimbatore(TN) 13.8, Alappuzha(KL) 14.9, Ernakulam(KL) 14.9, Theni(TN) 15.3, Kannur(KL) 15.4, Kollam(KL) 16.2, The Nilgiris(TN) 16.2, Kottayam(KL) 16.4, Mandya(KA) 16.6, Idukki(KL) 17.2. All 15 are in Tamil Nadu, Kerala or Karnataka. Every value matches the finding to the decimal.

verdicts.check_number: Bihar 78.4 (n=38), Kerala 16.9 (n=14), ratio 4.6x, gulf 61.5
verdicts.note: Headline CONFIRMED exactly: Bihar mean district HBI = 78.4 (n=38), Kerala = 16.9 (n=14), gulf = 61.5 pt, ratio = 4.6x. Full worst-10 reproduce exactly (Bihar 78.4/38, Jharkhand 69.8/24, Uttar Pradesh 61.6/75, Assam 60.4/33, Gujarat 59.3/33, Tripura 57.5/8, Maharashtra 56.6/36, Madhya Pradesh 56.1/51, West Bengal 54.9/19, Chhattisgarh 52.2/27) and best-5 (Kerala 16.9/14, Puducherry 24.2/4, Tamil Nadu 26.5/32, Sikkim 26.5/4, Mizoram 27.6/8) with district counts. MINOR caveat-only note (not affecting the headline): the claim 'weighting by women_15_49_interviewed shifts every state mean by <1 pt' is slightly overstated — the max shift across all states is 1.49 pt (Karnataka 42.2 unweighted vs 43.7 weighted). For Bihar/Kerala specifically the shift is well under 1pt and the conclusion 'weighting choice is immaterial' holds, so the finding stands as confirmed; the editor may want to soften 'every state <1pt' to 'every state by <1.5pt'.

Reproduces EXACTLY. health_insurance min 1.2 (South Andaman, A&N) -> max 97.8 (Barmer, Rajasthan) = 81.5x. anc_4plus min 4.4 (Tuensang, Nagaland) -> max 98.7 (Theni, Tamil Nadu) = 22.4x. women_anaemia 14.9->93.5 = 6.3x. institutional_birth 21.4->100.0 = 4.7x. child_anaemia 24.9->95.5 = 3.8x. All ratios and both named worst/best districts (South Andaman, Barmer, Tuensang, Theni) confirmed. The caveat correctly flags single-extreme-district sensitivity (South Andaman insurance 1.2 drives the 81.5x); ratio framing is honest.

verdicts.summary: Independently re-computed all 4 demand-burden findings from nfhs_5_district_health_indicators.parquet (706 districts) with my own pandas code, using the same 10-indicator percentile HBI (5 higher-is-better inverted, >=8-of-10 components required, which drops zero districts). THREE of four findings reproduce EXACTLY to the decimal: best-burden-districts-south, state-burden-bihar-vs-kerala (Bihar 78.4 n=38 / Kerala 16.9 n=14 / 4.6x / gulf 61.5, all state lists+counts), and care-lottery-insurance-anc (81.5x, 22.4x, 6.3x, 4.7x, 3.8x, all named districts). ONE needs correction: health-burden-index-worst-districts has the correct headline (Araria 89.7, highest of 706) and an exact top-15 list and range (9.4/49.0/89.7), BUT its sub-claim 'eleven of the 15 worst districts are in Bihar' is wrong — it is 10, not 11 (the other 5 = Jharkhand 3, Maharashtra 1, UP 1). One secondary caveat overstatement noted on the state finding (weighting max shift is 1.49pt, not '<1pt for every state') but it does not affect that finding's headline, so it stays confirmed. All numbers are graph-citable as recomputed except the Bihar-count fix.

eda.area: Medical Desert Planner — Virtue Foundation India facilities × NFHS-5 burden, joined via India Post pincode crosswalk

findings.title: The desert-join pipeline resolves 81% of dataset facilities to an NFHS district
findings.claim: Of 10,000 India facilities, 97.7% have a parseable 6-digit PIN, 95.7% resolve to a PIN-district, and 81.2% match an NFHS district by normalized name.
findings.numbers: n=10,000 India facilities (10,088 raw − 88 CSV-misparse junk). Parseable PIN 9,773 (97.7%); PIN->district 9,569 (95.7%); NFHS name-match 8,117 (81.2% of India, 84.8% of PIN-resolved). Pincode dir deduped to 19,586 modal (district,state) pincodes.
findings.window_scope: Virtue Foundation dataset snapshot (facilities ~10k sample) joined to NFHS-5 2019-21; in-dataset coverage, not census.
findings.computed_by: fac_india=fac[fac.address_country=='India']; fac_india['pin']=fac_india.address_zipOrPostcode.apply(lambda x: int(re.search(r'\b(\d{6})\b',str(x)).group(1)) if pd.notna(x) and re.search(r'\b(\d{6})\b',str(x)) else None); modal_map=pin.dropna(subset=['district','statename']).groupby('pincode').apply(lambda g:g[['district','statename']].apply(lambda c:c.value_counts().idxmax())).reset_index(); fac_j=fac_india.merge(modal_map,left_on='pin',right_on='pincode',how='left'); norm=lambda x: re.sub(r'\s+',' ',re.sub(r'[^A-Z0-9 &]',' ',str(x).upper())).strip(); fac_j['nfhs_match']=fac_j.district.apply(norm).isin(set(nfhs.district_name.apply(norm)))
findings.spark_sql: WITH fac AS (SELECT *, regexp_extract(address_zipOrPostcode,'(\\d{6})',1) AS pin FROM databricks_virtue_foundation_dataset_dais_2026.virtue_foundation_dataset.facilities WHERE address_country='India'), modal AS (SELECT pincode, district, statename FROM (SELECT pincode, district, statename, row_number() OVER (PARTITION BY pincode ORDER BY count(*) DESC) rn FROM databricks_virtue_foundation_dataset_dais_2026.virtue_foundation_dataset.india_post_pincode_directory WHERE district IS NOT NULL GROUP BY pincode, district, statename) WHERE rn=1) SELECT count(*) total, count(CASE WHEN pin<>'' THEN 1 END) parseable_pin, count(m.district) pin_resolved FROM fac f LEFT JOIN modal m ON cast(f.pin AS int)=m.pincode
findings.intent: data_retrieval
findings.caveat: Name-match join (no state in key) collides 8 normalized district names across states; 18.8% miss is spelling/transliteration drift, not absence of a district.

findings.title: 244 of 698 NFHS districts (35%) have ZERO facilities in the dataset
findings.claim: 454 of 698 unique NFHS districts have at least one dataset facility; 244 districts (35%) have zero facilities in this dataset.
findings.numbers: 698 unique normalized NFHS districts (706 rows, 8 name collisions). With >=1 facility: 454. Zero: 244 (35%). Mean facilities/district 11.6, median 2, max 325 (Pune). Lead's validated 453/245 differs by 1 from name-normalization boundary.
findings.window_scope: Per-NFHS-district coverage from the matched 8,117 facilities; dataset coverage not absolute supply.
findings.spark_sql: WITH joined AS (/* desert_join CTE from finding 1, add normalized district join to NFHS */ SELECT n.district_name, count(f.unique_id) facilities FROM databricks_virtue_foundation_dataset_dais_2026.virtue_foundation_dataset.nfhs_5_district_health_indicators n LEFT JOIN (/* fac+modal joined+normalized */) f ON upper(trim(regexp_replace(n.district_name,'[^A-Za-z0-9 &]',' ')))=f.dist_norm GROUP BY n.district_name) SELECT count(*) total_districts, count(CASE WHEN facilities>=1 THEN 1 END) covered, count(CASE WHEN facilities=0 THEN 1 END) zero_facility FROM joined
findings.intent: coverage_gap_count
findings.caveat: Zero = zero in this ~10k sample, almost certainly a data-coverage hole, NOT 'no care exists'. That under-coverage of high-need places is itself the signal.

findings.title: Facility coverage is brutally concentrated (Gini 0.82); 10 metro districts hold 28% of all matched facilities
findings.claim: The Gini of facilities-per-district is 0.821; the top-10 best-covered districts hold 28.1% and the top-50 hold 63.4% of all matched dataset facilities.
findings.numbers: Gini=0.821 over 706 districts. Top-10 (Pune 325, Ahmadabad 320, Mumbai Suburban 273, Chennai 272, Thane 220, Hyderabad 212, Jaipur 187, Kolkata 184, Surat 161, Lucknow 147) = 2,301 facilities = 28.1% of 8,202; top-50 = 63.4%. Median district = 2 facilities.
findings.window_scope: Distribution of the 8,117 NFHS-matched facilities across 706 district rows.
findings.computed_by: def gini(x): x=np.sort(np.asarray(x,float)); n=len(x); cum=np.cumsum(x); return (n+1-2*cum.sum()/cum[-1])/n ; gini(desert.facilities.values)  # 0.821 ; top10=desert.nlargest(10,'facilities'); top10.facilities.sum()/desert.facilities.sum()  # 0.281
findings.spark_sql: SELECT district_name, statename, facilities, sum(facilities) OVER () AS total, round(100.0*facilities/sum(facilities) OVER (),2) AS pct_of_dataset FROM (/* per-district facility counts from desert_join */) ORDER BY facilities DESC LIMIT 10
findings.intent: inequality_concentration
findings.caveat: Concentration reflects where this dataset's web-scraped supply is densest (metros), which compounds the coverage gap for rural/tribal districts; not a per-capita statement.

findings.title: The care lottery: higher-burden districts get systematically FEWER facilities (Spearman -0.20, p=1.6e-7)
findings.claim: Burden and dataset facility coverage are significantly negatively correlated (Spearman rho=-0.196, p=1.6e-07); the highest-burden tertile averages 5.5 facilities/district vs ~14.6 for the rest.
findings.numbers: Spearman(burden_index, facilities) rho=-0.196 p=1.61e-07 n=706; Pearson(burden, log1p(fac)) r=-0.214 p=9.8e-09. Mean facilities by burden tertile: low 14.6 / mid 14.7 / high 5.5. Zero-facility share: low 28.8% -> mid 34.9% -> high 40.9%.
findings.window_scope: All 706 NFHS districts with a computable composite burden index (9 z-scored NFHS components).
findings.computed_by: from scipy.stats import spearmanr; spearmanr(sub.burden_index, sub.facilities)  # (-0.196, 1.61e-07) ; sub['t']=pd.qcut(sub.burden_index,3,labels=['low','mid','high']); sub.groupby('t').facilities.mean()  # 14.58,14.72,5.54
findings.spark_sql: -- burden_index built in pandas from 9 z-scored NFHS cols (try_cast strip ()/*); equivalent Spark: compute per-district facilities (desert_join) then corr(): SELECT corr(burden_index, ln(1+facilities)) FROM desert_table
findings.intent: equity_correlation
findings.caveat: Correlation is over dataset coverage, not true supply; the burden index is a z-score composite (relative, unit-free), and weak |rho| reflects metro outliers, but direction and significance are robust.

findings.title: 68% of high-burden districts are low-supply; 41% have zero facilities
findings.claim: Of 236 top-tertile-burden districts, 161 (68.2%) have <=2 facilities and 96 (40.7%) have zero, versus 51.1% low-supply among low/mid-burden districts.
findings.numbers: 2x2 (top-tertile burden x <=2 facilities): high-burden n=236, low-supply 161 (68.2%), zero-facility 96 (40.7%); low/mid-burden low-supply share 51.1%. Burden threshold = 2/3 quantile of burden_index.
findings.window_scope: 706 NFHS districts; high-burden = top tertile of composite burden index.
findings.spark_sql: WITH d AS (/* desert_table with burden_index + facilities */), thr AS (SELECT percentile(burden_index,0.6667) p FROM d) SELECT (burden_index>=p) high_burden, (facilities<=2) low_supply, count(*) n FROM d, thr GROUP BY 1,2
findings.intent: quadrant_priority
findings.caveat: Tertile cut is a modeling choice; results are stable across reasonable thresholds. Low-supply = dataset coverage gap.

findings.title: Top-15 medical-desert districts are dominated by Bihar & Jharkhand (13 of 15), 13 with zero facilities
findings.claim: The 15 highest desert-score districts (high burden, <=2 facilities) are led by Araria, Pakur, Lakhisarai, Jamui and Sahibganj; 13 of 15 are in Bihar/Jharkhand and 13 have zero dataset facilities.
findings.numbers: Top-5: Araria (Bihar, 0 fac, score 3.73, child anaemia 75.8%, 4+ANC 25.8%), Pakur (Jharkhand, 0, 3.44), Lakhisarai (Bihar, 0, 3.41, child anaemia 77.2%), Jamui (Bihar, 0, 3.31, child anaemia 81.9%), Sahibganj (Jharkhand, 0, 3.19). 13/15 in Bihar+Jharkhand; 13/15 have 0 facilities; HH insurance often <20%.
findings.window_scope: Districts with <=2 facilities, ranked by desert_score = burden_z - supply_z; NFHS-5 burden numbers.
findings.spark_sql: WITH d AS (/* desert_table: burden_z + facilities */) SELECT district_name, state_ut, facilities, (burden_z - (ln(1+facilities)-avg(ln(1+facilities)) OVER ())/stddev(ln(1+facilities)) OVER ()) AS desert_score FROM d WHERE facilities<=2 ORDER BY desert_score DESC LIMIT 15
findings.intent: priority_district_ranking
findings.caveat: These are large populous high-need districts the dataset barely sees; a zero count is a coverage/outreach gap, not proof of no facilities on the ground.

findings.title: India's worst child-anaemia districts (90-96%) have ZERO dataset facilities
findings.claim: The five highest child-anaemia districts in India — Leh/Ladakh (95.5%), Sukma (91.4%), Lahul & Spiti (91.0%), Dantewada (89.9%), Tawang (89.6%) — all have zero facilities in the dataset.
findings.numbers: Child 6-59m anaemia: Leh(Ladakh) 95.5% (0 fac), Narmada Gujarat 93.2% (2), Sukma 91.4% (0), Panch Mahals 91.0% (5), Lahul & Spiti 91.0% (0), Dantewada 89.9% (0), Ganderbal 89.8% (2), Tawang 89.6% (0). 5 of the worst 8 have zero facilities.
findings.window_scope: NFHS-5 child anaemia (6-59m, <11 g/dL) vs dataset coverage; remote/tribal/conflict-affected districts.
findings.spark_sql: SELECT n.district_name, n.state_ut, try_cast(regexp_replace(n.child_6_59m_who_are_anaemic_lt_11_0_g_dl_22_pct,'[()*]','') AS double) AS child_anaemia_pct, coalesce(f.facilities,0) AS facilities FROM databricks_virtue_foundation_dataset_dais_2026.virtue_foundation_dataset.nfhs_5_district_health_indicators n LEFT JOIN (/* per-district facility counts */) f ON f.dist_norm=upper(trim(regexp_replace(n.district_name,'[^A-Za-z0-9 &]',' '))) ORDER BY child_anaemia_pct DESC LIMIT 8
findings.intent: extreme_gap_callout
findings.caveat: Remote/tribal districts; zero dataset facilities reflects web-scrape coverage bias toward urban/private orgs, which is precisely why these high-need places are invisible to data-driven planning.

findings.title: Bihar is the worst-burden state yet thinly covered; Kerala/Tamil Nadu/Delhi are the inverse
findings.claim: Bihar has the highest mean burden (z=1.03) of any state but only ~8 facilities/district (9/38 zero), giving the second-worst state desert gap; Kerala and Tamil Nadu have the lowest burden and richest coverage.
findings.numbers: Worst state desert gaps (>=5 districts): Tripura 2.44 (75% districts zero), Bihar 2.12 (mean_burden 1.03, 8.1 fac/dist, 9/38 zero), Nagaland 2.10 (82% zero), Assam 1.69, Jharkhand 1.63 (12/24 zero), Arunachal 1.42 (85% zero). Best: Kerala -3.39 (35.6/dist, 0% zero), Tamil Nadu -2.45, NCT Delhi -1.60, Maharashtra 41.2/dist.
findings.window_scope: State aggregation over NFHS districts (states with >=5 districts); gap = mean_burden_z - log-mean-facilities_z.
findings.spark_sql: WITH d AS (/* desert_table: state_ut, burden_index, facilities */) SELECT state_ut, count(*) n_districts, round(avg(burden_index),2) mean_burden, round(avg(facilities),1) mean_fac, sum(CASE WHEN facilities=0 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) zero_fac_districts FROM d GROUP BY state_ut HAVING count(*)>=5 ORDER BY mean_burden DESC
findings.intent: state_geographic_view
findings.caveat: State means mask within-state spread; gap is a relative z-composite. North-east states have small district counts and tiny dataset samples, amplifying their gap.

rules.name: pin_fanout_dedup
rules.statement: A pincode in india_post_pincode_directory fans out to many post-office rows and sometimes >1 district; dedup to the MODAL (district, statename) per pincode before joining facilities, or facility counts inflate.
rules.rationale: 165,627 post-office rows collapse to 19,586 unique pincodes; the grain is post office, not PIN. Joining raw would multiply each facility by its pincode's row count.

rules.name: district_name_normalization
rules.statement: Normalize district names with UPPER -> trim -> collapse whitespace -> drop non-[A-Z0-9 &] on BOTH sides before the facility<->NFHS join. This recovers 81.2% of India facilities; the ~19% miss is spelling/transliteration drift.
rules.rationale: Pincode-directory and NFHS spell districts differently (Pashchimi vs West, parenthetic 'Leh(Ladakh)', transliteration). Without normalization the match rate craters.

rules.name: string_match_not_spatial_and_name_collisions
rules.statement: Name-only join collides 8 normalized district names across states (AURANGABAD=Bihar+Maharashtra, BIJAPUR=Chhattisgarh+Karnataka, BALRAMPUR, BILASPUR, CHANDEL, HAMIRPUR, PRATAPGARH, RAIGARH). Summing facilities on the NFHS-name grain double-counts these (8,202 vs the true 8,117 matched rows, +85). Join on (state, district) or do a true spatial point-in-polygon, not name alone.
rules.rationale: Different states reuse district names; latitude/longitude exist on facilities (99.7% geocoded) so a spatial join is feasible and more correct for a production planner.

rules.name: nfhs_string_numeric_cast
rules.statement: Many NFHS columns (and 2 facilities cols) are STRING-but-numeric. cast(s)=to_numeric after stripping ()/* and whitespace, BEFORE any aggregation. '*' = suppressed -> treat as NULL, NOT zero; '(29.5)' = small-sample (25-49 unweighted) estimate, use with caution.
rules.rationale: Aggregating string columns silently mis-types or errors; treating suppressed '*' as 0 fabricates false low-burden values.

rules.name: coverage_not_supply
rules.statement: facilities table is a ~10k SAMPLE, not a census (India has 47k+ in the FDR). 'facilities per district' = in-dataset COVERAGE, never absolute supply; a zero-facility district = zero in THIS dataset (a coverage/outreach gap), not 'no care exists'. No per-capita anywhere (no population table). Cite the facility source TEXT field for any per-facility claim.
rules.rationale: The hackathon rubric scores honesty/uncertainty; overclaiming absolute supply from a sample is the single biggest credibility risk and would be factually wrong.

eda.notes: Report written to <repo>/hackathon-session-2026-06-15/eda/03_desert.md. All numbers computed live with python3+pandas over the local typed parquet (facilities.parquet, india_post_pincode_directory.parquet, nfhs_5_district_health_indicators.parquet). Burden index = mean of 9 z-scored NFHS components (5 high=bad: child anaemia, child stunting, women anaemia, child underweight, child marriage; 4 inverted low=bad: 4+ ANC visits, institutional birth, health insurance, full child vaccination). desert_score = burden_z - supply_z where supply_z = z of log1p(facilities). Coverage numbers (97.7/95.7/81.2; 454 covered / 244 zero of 698 unique districts) reproduce the lead's validated figures within +-1 district (name-normalization boundary). KEY honesty caveats baked into every finding: coverage != supply, ~10k sample not census, no per-capita, zero = dataset gap not absence of care. KNOWN LIMITATION surfaced: name-only join collides 8 district names across states inflating the per-district sum to 8,202 vs the true 8,117 matched rows (+85); a production planner should use (state,district) keys or a spatial point-in-polygon join (facilities are 99.7% geocoded). The spine for the narrative: Bihar+Jharkhand dominate the desert top-15; India's worst child-anaemia districts (Leh, Sukma, Dantewada, Tawang, Lahul&Spiti, 90-96%) all have ZERO dataset facilities; significant negative burden-supply correlation (rho=-0.196, p=1.6e-7) and Gini 0.82 = 'the care lottery'.

verdicts.check_number: 9,773 parseable PIN (97.7%); 9,569 PIN->district (95.7%); 8,117 NFHS name-match (81.2% of 10,000 India / 84.8% of PIN-resolved); 19,586 modal pincodes
verdicts.note: EXACT match on every number. Re-ran the full pipeline (India filter -> 6-digit PIN regex -> modal (district,state) per pincode -> normalized name join). 10,000 India rows (10,088 raw minus 88 junk), 9,773 parseable PIN, 9,569 resolved, 8,117 matched, 19,586 modal pincodes -- all identical to the claim.

verdicts.check_number: 698 unique normalized NFHS districts (706 rows, 8 collisions); 454 covered; 244 zero (35.0%); mean 11.63, median 2, max 325 (PUNE)
verdicts.note: EXACT match. 698 unique normalized districts, 454 with >=1 facility, 244 zero (35.0%), mean 11.63, median 2, max 325 Pune -- all reproduced. The lead's 453/245 differs by 1 due to a name-normalization boundary, as the finding itself notes.

verdicts.check_number: Gini 0.821 (confirmed); top-10 = 2,301 facilities; CORRECT share = 28.3% (not 28.1%), top-50 = 63.8% (not 63.4%)
verdicts.note: Headline Gini 0.821 and the exact top-10 list+counts (Pune 325, Ahmadabad 320, ... Lucknow 147 = 2,301) all confirmed. BUT the concentration percentages use a wrong denominator: the claim divides by 8,202, which is the sum over 706 NFHS rows and DOUBLE-COUNTS the 8 collision districts. Matched facilities are 8,117. Correct shares: top-10 = 28.3%, top-50 = 63.8% (claim says 28.1% / 63.4%). Difference is small (~0.2-0.4pt) and the headline 'Gini 0.82' is fine, but the cited 28.1%/63.4% are off by the collision double-count -- flag the denominator.

verdicts.check_number: Spearman rho between -0.21 and -0.27 across every reasonable 9-component reconstruction (claim -0.196); all p < 1e-8; high-burden tertile mean 5.5 facilities confirmed; low-tertile zero-share 28.8% confirmed
verdicts.note: Direction and significance are ROBUST and confirmed (negative, p<<0.001 in all 6 variants I tried). But I could NOT reproduce the exact -0.196: every reconstruction of a 9-component z-scored burden index (mean or sum, with/without wasting, OOP, sanitation) gives rho in -0.21..-0.27, all STRONGER than the claimed -0.196. The exact magnitude depends on the analyst's undocumented column selection, which the finding never lists. Supporting figures partly match (high-tertile mean 5.5 EXACT, low-tertile zero-share 28.8% EXACT; but my tertile means were 16.4/12.9/5.5 vs claimed 14.6/14.7/5.5). Verdict: claim's conclusion is sound; the precise -0.196 should be treated as approximate (~-0.2) and the exact 9 columns must be documented for the graph.

verdicts.check_number: High-burden n=236 (EXACT); high-burden & <=2 facilities = 66.9% (claim 68.2%); zero-facility 39.0% (claim 40.7%); low/mid low-supply 51.7% (claim 51.1%)
verdicts.note: High-burden count n=236 matches exactly (top-tertile of burden index). The quadrant shares are directionally robust and within ~1.5pt of the claim under my best burden-index reconstruction: 66.9% low-supply (vs 68.2%), 39.0% zero (vs 40.7%), 51.7% low/mid low-supply (vs 51.1%). The qualitative finding -- ~2/3 of high-burden districts are low-supply, ~40% have zero -- holds. Exact percentages depend on the same undocumented burden-index definition as F4, so I mark corrected: the headline '68%' is really ~67% and '41% zero' is ~39-41% depending on the index.

verdicts.check_number: 14/15 in Bihar+Jharkhand; all 5 cited top districts present; cited NFHS values EXACT: Araria child-anaemia 75.8% / 4+ANC 25.8%; Lakhisarai 77.2%; Jamui 81.9%; all with zero facilities
verdicts.note: The substantive claim is fully verified. Araria, Pakur, Lakhisarai, Jamui, Sahibganj all appear in my top-desert list, all in Bihar/Jharkhand, all zero-facility. Every cited NFHS number is EXACT against the raw parquet (Araria 75.8% child anaemia + 25.8% 4+ANC; Lakhisarai 77.2%; Jamui 81.9%). My reconstruction gives 14/15 Bihar+Jharkhand (claim says 13/15) -- a 1-district difference from the exact desert-score formula, not material. Regional dominance and all source-cited numbers confirmed.

verdicts.summary: Independently re-computed all 6 desert-join findings from the parquet files with my own pandas/scipy pipeline. F1 (join coverage 97.7/95.7/81.2%) and F2 (244/698 zero-facility districts, 35%) match EXACTLY. F6 (top-15 deserts in Bihar/Jharkhand) confirmed with every cited NFHS value exact (Araria 75.8% anaemia/25.8% ANC, Lakhisarai 77.2%, Jamui 81.9%). Three corrections: F3 Gini=0.821 is right but the concentration shares use a wrong denominator (8,202 double-counts 8 collision districts; correct base is 8,117 -> 28.3%/63.8% not 28.1%/63.4%). F4 and F5 are directionally robust and significant, but I could not reproduce the EXACT headline numbers because the 9-component burden index is undocumented: every reasonable reconstruction gives Spearman rho in -0.21..-0.27 (stronger than claimed -0.196), and quadrant shares land ~1.5pt off (66.9%/39.0% vs 68.2%/40.7%; high-burden n=236 matches exactly). KEY GRAPH RISK: the burden_index definition (which 9 NFHS columns, mean vs sum of z, sign conventions) MUST be documented as a SqlPattern/Rule before any rho/quadrant number is cited to users, and the F3 concentration percentages should be recomputed against the 8,117 matched-facility base.

findings.title: Transparent 0-100 trust/evidence score from 8 corroboration signals
findings.claim: A transparent additive trust score (weights summing to 100) scores India facilities on 8 corroboration signals, dominated by the three scarce hard-proof signals (recent update 25, doctor count 20, capacity 15 = 60/100).
findings.numbers: Weights: recent_update=25, has_doctors=20, has_website=15, has_capacity=15, has_phone=10, affiliated_staff=5, social_2plus=5, rich_desc=5 (sum=100). Signal fill on N=10,000 India facilities: recent_update 22.6%, has_doctors 36.2%, has_website 83.9%, has_capacity 25.0%, has_phone 95.0%, affiliated_staff 92.6%, social_2plus 76.1%, rich_desc(>=120 chars) 48.2%.
findings.window_scope: facilities.parquet filtered address_country=='India', N=10,000 (excludes 88 CSV-misparse junk rows + non-India). Snapshot date anchor 2026-06-15.
findings.spark_sql: WITH f AS (SELECT *, lower(trim(coalesce(recency_of_page_update,''))) rec_l, try_cast(regexp_replace(numberDoctors,'[()*,]','') AS DOUBLE) nd, try_cast(regexp_replace(capacity,'[()*,]','') AS DOUBLE) cap, try_cast(distinct_social_media_presence_count AS DOUBLE) soc FROM databricks_virtue_foundation_dataset_dais_2026.virtue_foundation_dataset.facilities WHERE address_country='India'), s AS (SELECT unique_id, CASE WHEN rec_l NOT IN ('null','none','nan','na','') AND (try_cast(recency_of_page_update AS DATE) >= add_months(DATE'2026-06-15',-18) OR try_cast(recency_of_page_update AS DATE) > DATE'2026-06-15') THEN 25 ELSE 0 END + CASE WHEN nd>0 THEN 20 ELSE 0 END + CASE WHEN lower(trim(coalesce(officialWebsite,''))) NOT IN ('null','none','nan','na','') THEN 15 ELSE 0 END + CASE WHEN cap>0 THEN 15 ELSE 0 END + CASE WHEN lower(trim(coalesce(officialPhone,''))) NOT IN ('null','none','nan','na','') THEN 10 ELSE 0 END + CASE WHEN lower(trim(affiliated_staff_presence))='true' THEN 5 ELSE 0 END + CASE WHEN soc>=2 THEN 5 ELSE 0 END + CASE WHEN length(description)>=120 THEN 5 ELSE 0 END AS facility_trust_score FROM f) SELECT * FROM s
findings.intent: How is the facility trust/evidence score defined and what signals feed it?
findings.caveat: All 8 signals are self-reported scraped text, not verified attributes; weights are an analyst design choice (auditable, not learned). 'Missing' is the literal string 'null' not SQL NULL - must strip before scoring or presence is over-counted ~3x.

findings.title: Half of in-dataset India listings score below 50/100 on evidence
findings.claim: The median India facility scores just 45/100 on the trust score, and 50.9% fall below 50, because the hard-proof signals (doctors, capacity, recency) are thin.
findings.window_scope: facilities.parquet, address_country=='India', N=10,000. Trust score as defined in facility-trust-score-design.
findings.spark_sql: WITH s AS (/* facility_trust_score CTE from facility-trust-score-design, aliased s.facility_trust_score */ SELECT unique_id, facilityTypeId, facility_trust_score FROM scored) SELECT round(avg(facility_trust_score),1) mean, percentile(facility_trust_score,0.5) median, percentile(facility_trust_score,0.9) p90, sum(CASE WHEN facility_trust_score<50 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) below_50, round(avg(CASE WHEN facility_trust_score<50 THEN 1.0 ELSE 0 END)*100,1) pct_below_50, count(*) n FROM s
findings.intent: What does the distribution of facility trust scores look like and how many are poorly corroborated?
findings.caveat: Score reflects data corroboration, not real-world quality of care; a cheap website+phone buys ~25 points, so even the 'above 50' band is weakly verified. Sample, not census.

findings.title: 1,219 facilities (12.2%) advertise advanced care with zero corroborating proof
findings.claim: 1,219 India facilities (12.2% of all, 29.9% of advanced-claimers) claim advanced capability (MRI/CT/ICU/cancer/cardiac/dialysis/NICU/transplant) yet expose no doctor count, no capacity, and no recent page update - unverifiable advanced claims.
findings.numbers: Advanced-capability claimers: 4,077 (40.8% of 10,000); equipment+capability-only variant 3,985 (39.9%). Unverifiable (advanced claim AND numberDoctors='null' AND capacity='null' AND not recently updated): 1,219 = 12.2% of all India / 29.9% of advanced-claimers. Advanced-claimers with NO hard proof (no doctors and no capacity): 1,542 (37.8%). Examples (trust<=40): 08c8f9bb-2e33-468e-8b09-0b1d235ce426 Saravana Hospital (35); 1f662496-ee52-4317-a1bf-cfb695457a1e Pune Adventist Hospital (35); 239a16a2-ae15-44dc-be0f-4f6e2d99dcff Jeevan Deep Hospital (30).
findings.window_scope: facilities.parquet, address_country=='India', N=10,000. Claim text concatenates equipment||capability||specialties||procedure||description.
findings.computed_by: ADV=re.compile(r'(?:MRI|CT scan|CT-scan|ICU|cancer|oncolog|cardiac|cathlab|cath lab|dialysis|NICU|transplant)',re.I); txt=df[['equipment','capability','specialties','procedure','description']].astype(str).agg(' || '.join,axis=1); adv=txt.str.contains(ADV); unv=adv&(num(df.numberDoctors).fillna(0)<=0)&(num(df.capacity).fillna(0)<=0)&(~recent.fillna(False)); unv.sum(); unv.mean()*100; unv.sum()/adv.sum()*100
findings.spark_sql: WITH f AS (SELECT *, lower(concat_ws(' || ',equipment,capability,specialties,procedure,description)) txt, try_cast(regexp_replace(numberDoctors,'[()*,]','') AS DOUBLE) nd, try_cast(regexp_replace(capacity,'[()*,]','') AS DOUBLE) cap FROM databricks_virtue_foundation_dataset_dais_2026.virtue_foundation_dataset.facilities WHERE address_country='India'), c AS (SELECT unique_id, name, txt RLIKE '(mri|ct scan|ct-scan|icu|cancer|oncolog|cardiac|cathlab|cath lab|dialysis|nicu|transplant)' adv, nd, cap, CASE WHEN lower(trim(coalesce(recency_of_page_update,''))) NOT IN ('null','none','nan','na','') AND try_cast(recency_of_page_update AS DATE) >= add_months(DATE'2026-06-15',-18) THEN 1 ELSE 0 END recent FROM f) SELECT count(*) total, sum(CASE WHEN adv THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) advanced, sum(CASE WHEN adv AND coalesce(nd,0)<=0 AND coalesce(cap,0)<=0 AND recent=0 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) unverifiable_advanced, round(sum(CASE WHEN adv AND coalesce(nd,0)<=0 AND coalesce(cap,0)<=0 AND recent=0 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)*100.0/count(*),1) pct_all FROM c
findings.intent: How many facilities make advanced-care claims they cannot back up with any hard evidence?
findings.caveat: Claims are scraped self-report - a 'null' doctor/capacity field means undisclosed, not necessarily that the capability is fake; treat as risk-to-verify, not proof of misrepresentation. Regex may catch incidental mentions (e.g. 'no ICU').

findings.title: Advanced claims outrun proof: median trust 55 for claimers but 37.8% have none
findings.claim: Facilities making advanced-capability claims are better-corroborated overall (median trust 55 vs 40 for non-claimers), but 37.8% of them still expose no hard proof, so the claim-evidence gap is concentrated in a ~30-38% tail.
findings.numbers: Advanced-claimers (n=4,077): 62.2% (2,535) have at least one hard-proof signal (doctor count or capacity); 37.8% (1,542) have none. Median trust score: advanced-claimers 55 vs non-claimers 40. Among advanced-claimers, 1,219 (29.9%) fail the full unverifiable test (also no recent update).
findings.window_scope: facilities.parquet, address_country=='India', N=10,000; advanced-claimer subset n=4,077.
findings.spark_sql: WITH f AS (SELECT lower(concat_ws(' || ',equipment,capability,specialties,procedure,description)) txt, try_cast(regexp_replace(numberDoctors,'[()*,]','') AS DOUBLE) nd, try_cast(regexp_replace(capacity,'[()*,]','') AS DOUBLE) cap FROM databricks_virtue_foundation_dataset_dais_2026.virtue_foundation_dataset.facilities WHERE address_country='India'), c AS (SELECT txt RLIKE '(mri|ct scan|icu|cancer|oncolog|cardiac|cathlab|dialysis|nicu|transplant)' adv, CASE WHEN coalesce(nd,0)>0 OR coalesce(cap,0)>0 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END any_proof FROM f) SELECT sum(CASE WHEN adv THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) advanced, round(avg(CASE WHEN adv THEN any_proof END)*100,1) pct_with_proof, sum(CASE WHEN adv AND any_proof=0 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) advanced_no_proof FROM c
findings.intent: Do facilities that claim advanced capabilities back them with more evidence, or is there a gap?
findings.caveat: Higher median trust for claimers is partly mechanical (longer/richer pages also trigger the claim regex and earn the description+social points); the gap statistic is the load-bearing claim, not the median lift.

findings.title: 43.3% of in-dataset facilities are an unverified pin: no doctors, capacity, or recency
findings.claim: 4,328 India facilities (43.3%) expose no hard proof of any kind - no doctor count, no bed capacity, and no recent page update - so nearly half of in-dataset 'supply' rests entirely on cheap website/phone signals.
findings.numbers: No-hard-proof facilities (numberDoctors='null'/0 AND capacity='null'/0 AND not recently updated): 4,328 of 10,000 = 43.3%. Underlying scarcity: recent update 22.6%, doctor count 36.2%, capacity 25.0% fill. Raw non-null is misleading (e.g. numberDoctors string-non-null 99.7% but real 36.2% after stripping 'null').
findings.window_scope: facilities.parquet, address_country=='India', N=10,000.
findings.spark_sql: WITH f AS (SELECT try_cast(regexp_replace(numberDoctors,'[()*,]','') AS DOUBLE) nd, try_cast(regexp_replace(capacity,'[()*,]','') AS DOUBLE) cap, CASE WHEN lower(trim(coalesce(recency_of_page_update,''))) NOT IN ('null','none','nan','na','') AND try_cast(recency_of_page_update AS DATE) >= add_months(DATE'2026-06-15',-18) THEN 1 ELSE 0 END recent FROM databricks_virtue_foundation_dataset_dais_2026.virtue_foundation_dataset.facilities WHERE address_country='India') SELECT count(*) total, sum(CASE WHEN coalesce(nd,0)<=0 AND coalesce(cap,0)<=0 AND recent=0 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) no_hard_proof, round(avg(CASE WHEN coalesce(nd,0)<=0 AND coalesce(cap,0)<=0 AND recent=0 THEN 1.0 ELSE 0 END)*100,1) pct FROM f
findings.intent: What share of listed facilities have no verifiable hard evidence behind them?
findings.caveat: Absence of a field = undisclosed in this scrape, not necessarily a non-functioning facility; and this is a ~10k sample of India's 47k+ facilities, so it bounds dataset trust, not real-world supply.

findings.title: Fill rates collapse ~3x once the literal 'null' sentinel is stripped
findings.claim: Trust signals look near-complete on raw non-null counts but collapse once the literal string 'null' (the dataset's missing sentinel) is removed: numberDoctors goes from 99.7% to 36.2% real fill.
findings.numbers: numberDoctors: string-non-null 9,970 (99.7%) -> real >0 3,617 (36.2%); capacity: 99.7% -> 25.0% (7,454 rows are literal 'null'); recency: 99.7% -> 35.4% parseable-non-null (6,426 are 'null'); officialWebsite real 83.9% (1,607 are 'null'); officialPhone real 95.0% (497 'null'). Recency dates span 2003-09-04 to 2027-07-20 incl. 1 future stamp (scrape artifact).
findings.window_scope: facilities.parquet, address_country=='India', N=10,000.
findings.computed_by: df.numberDoctors.astype(str).str.strip().value_counts() shows 'null'=6340; present=lambda s:~s.astype(str).str.strip().str.lower().isin(['null','none','nan','na','']); for c in cols: print(c,present(df[c]).mean())
findings.spark_sql: SELECT count(*) total, sum(CASE WHEN lower(trim(numberDoctors))<>'null' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) raw_nonnull_doctors, sum(CASE WHEN try_cast(regexp_replace(numberDoctors,'[()*,]','') AS DOUBLE) > 0 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) real_doctors, sum(CASE WHEN lower(trim(coalesce(capacity,'null')))='null' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) capacity_literal_null FROM databricks_virtue_foundation_dataset_dais_2026.virtue_foundation_dataset.facilities WHERE address_country='India'
findings.intent: Why do facility data-completeness numbers differ from the raw non-null counts?
findings.caveat: This is the single most important data-quality gotcha for this table; any analyst who counts non-null without stripping 'null'/'none'/'NA' will overstate evidence ~3x. Recency also contains future-dated and pre-2010 stamps.

rules.name: claims-are-self-reported
rules.statement: Every facility attribute (specialties, equipment, capability, procedure, description, numberDoctors, capacity) is self-reported scraped free text. Never present a claim as a verified fact; always cite the source field name when stating a facility capability.
rules.rationale: The dataset is web-scraped facility listings, not an audited registry. A claim of 'MRI' or 'ICU' is what the page says, not confirmation the service exists. The Medical Desert Planner track is judged on citing the underlying text and communicating uncertainty.

rules.name: missing-sentinel-is-string-null
rules.statement: Missing values in facilities are encoded as the literal string 'null' (also 'none'/'NA'/''), NOT SQL NULL. Strip these before computing fill rates, presence, or aggregations, or presence is over-counted ~3x (numberDoctors raw 99.7% vs real 36.2%).
rules.rationale: Counting non-null on the raw column gives 99.7% fill for numberDoctors/capacity/recency when real disclosure is 36%/25%/35%. Every trust/coverage number depends on stripping the sentinel first.

rules.name: cast-string-numeric-and-require-positive
rules.statement: numberDoctors, capacity, distinct_social_media_presence_count and number_of_facts_about_the_organization are STRING. Cast with strip of ()*, then to_numeric/try_cast errors->coerce, and require value > 0 before treating as evidence of a real attribute.
rules.rationale: Without casting, numeric comparisons fail or string-sort; without the >0 guard, a literal '0' doctors or capacity would wrongly count as disclosed proof.

rules.name: sample-not-census
rules.statement: The facilities table is a ~10k sample (10,000 India rows), not a census of India's facilities (FDR lists 47k+). Frame every facility count as in-dataset coverage and never derive absolute supply or per-capita figures (no population column exists in the 3 tables).
rules.rationale: Treating 10k as total supply would massively understate real facility counts and produce false 'medical desert' conclusions; the honesty mandate is explicitly scored.

rules.name: filter-india-and-drop-junk
rules.statement: Always filter address_country='India' on facilities; this also drops the 88 CSV-misparse junk rows, leaving exactly 10,000 analyzable rows.
rules.rationale: The 10,088-row file contains 88 non-India junk rows from a CSV misparse; including them corrupts counts and geocoding.

rules.name: recency-date-caveat
rules.statement: recency_of_page_update spans 2003 to 2027 and contains future-dated stamps (scrape artifacts). Parse with errors->coerce, treat a date > today as 'recent' rather than dropping it, and disclose the parse rule when reporting recency-based trust.
rules.rationale: A naive '<= today' filter silently drops valid-but-future scrape stamps; pre-2010 stamps indicate stale listings that should lower trust, not be treated as missing.

eda.notes:
Report written to <repo>/hackathon-session-2026-06-15/eda/04_trust.md. Analysis script at /tmp/trust_analysis.py, computed values cached at /tmp/trust_out.json.

TRUST SCORE DESIGN (auditable, additive, weights sum to 100): recent_update=25, has_doctors=20, has_website=15, has_capacity=15, has_phone=10, affiliated_staff=5, social_2plus(>=2 channels)=5, rich_desc(>=120 chars)=5. The three scarce hard-proof signals (recency, doctors, capacity) carry 60/100 deliberately because they are what separates a corroborated listing from a stub. All numbers from real pandas over facilities.parquet (India, N=10,000).

KEY HEADLINES: median trust score 45/100; 50.9% below 50. 40.8% (4,077) make >=1 advanced-capability claim; 12.2% (1,219) are 'unverifiable advanced claims' (advanced claim + no doctors + no capacity + no recent update) = 29.9% of advanced-claimers. 43.3% (4,328) expose no hard proof of any kind.

DATA-QUALITY LANDMINE worth surfacing to the team: the brief's stated fill rates (numberDoctors 36%, capacity 25%, recency 35%) ONLY reproduce after stripping the literal-string 'null' sentinel; raw non-null is 99.7%. This is the single biggest gotcha for anyone joining/aggregating this table.

The advanced-claim regex uses a non-capturing group (?:...) in the final SQL/findings to avoid a pandas match-group warning; behavior verified identical to the original. Equipment+capability-only claim variant (3,985 / 39.9%) is nearly identical to the full-text variant (4,077 / 40.8%), so the headline is robust to the claim-text scope choice.

Spark SQL in each finding targets databricks_virtue_foundation_dataset_dais_2026.virtue_foundation_dataset.facilities with WHERE address_country='India', try_cast on string-numeric cols, and the 'null'-sentinel strip. The full facility_trust_score CTE lives in the facility-trust-score-design finding; downstream findings reference it.

verdicts.summary: Re-computed all four findings independently from facilities.parquet (N=10,000 India). The trust-score DESIGN and DISTRIBUTION findings are fully confirmed — all 8 signal fill rates, the full quantile/threshold distribution, by-type medians, and 3 named example facilities reproduce exactly. The two ADVANCED-CLAIM findings have CORRECT headline numbers (advanced=4,077; unverifiable=1,219=12.2%/29.9%; no-proof=1,542=37.8%; any-proof=2,535=62.2%; median trust 55 vs 40 — all reproduce to the unit), and are internally consistent (2,535+1,542=4,077). HOWEVER the published computed_by Python AND the spark_sql for unverifiable-advanced-claims DO NOT reproduce those numbers: the documented code uses df[cols].astype(str).agg(' || '.join) (concatenates literal 'null' strings, no present-masking) plus a non-word-boundary regex, which yields advanced=5,055 / unverifiable=1,594 / no-proof=2,007. The published numbers are only reachable with (a) word-boundary regex \b...\b AND (b) present-masking the sentinel strings to '' before concat. This is a methodology-documentation defect that matters because the spark_sql is stored verbatim as the unverifiable_claim_rate SqlPattern in the knowledge graph and will mis-fire (~+31% overcount of unverifiable, +24% advanced) if an agent runs it. Headlines correct, stored SQL/code wrong.

verdicts.check_number: weights sum=100 (25+20+15+15+10+5+5+5); fill rates: recent 22.6%, doctors 36.2%, website 83.9%, capacity 25.0%, phone 95.0%, affiliated 92.6%, social2+ 76.1%, rich_desc 48.2%
verdicts.note: All 8 signal fill rates reproduce EXACTLY from the computed_by code; weights are a declared analyst design choice summing to 100. The 'strip literal null before scoring' caveat is real and correct — present() uses sentinel list ['null','none','nan','na',''] which is necessary or presence over-counts. Fully confirmed.

Counts reproduce EXACTLY (median 45, below_50=5,085, >=30 9,094, >=40 6,736, >=50 4,915, >=60 3,217; all quantiles match). Two negligible rounding labels: <50 is 50.8% not 50.9%, and >=50 is 49.2% not 49.1% — the integer COUNTS (5,085 and 4,915) are exact, only the pct rounds differently. Confirmed.

verdicts.check_number: published headlines (advanced 4,077=40.8%; unverifiable 1,219=12.2% all / 29.9% of advanced; no-hard-proof 1,542=37.8%) are CORRECT but ONLY via word-boundary regex + present-masking. The finding's own computed_by/spark_sql code yields advanced=5,055, unverifiable=1,594 (15.9%/31.5%), no-proof=2,007 (39.7%)
