verdicts.note: Headline NUMBERS reproduce to the unit and the 3 named examples + trust scores (Saravana 35, Pune Adventist 35, Jeevan Deep 30) all match. BUT the documented method is wrong: computed_by does df[cols].astype(str).agg(' || '.join) with no present-masking (so literal 'null' tokens stay in the text) and ADV=re.compile(...) WITHOUT \b word boundaries — that produces 5,055 advanced-claimers, not 4,077 (the regex incidentally hits substrings like 'icu' inside other words and counts 'null'-filled rows). Correct method = re.compile(r'\b(...)\b', re.I) on a concat where sentinel fields are masked to ''. The spark_sql will be stored as the unverifiable_claim_rate SqlPattern and will OVERCOUNT (+31% unverifiable, +24% advanced). The 'equip+cap-only variant 3,985' sub-number IS reproducible from the simple code. Marking corrected: right answer, wrong/non-reproducible documented method that must be fixed before the SqlPattern enters the graph.

verdicts.check_number: 62.2% (2,535) have >=1 hard-proof signal, 37.8% (1,542) none, n=4,077; median trust 55 (claimers) vs 40 (non); 1,219 (29.9%) fail full unverifiable test — all reproduce EXACTLY but only under word-boundary+present-masked advanced_claim definition
verdicts.note: Every number reproduces exactly (2,535/4,077=62.2%, 1,542/4,077=37.8%, median 55 vs 40, 1,219=29.9%) and is internally consistent (2,535+1,542=4,077). Same defect as unverifiable-advanced-claims: depends on a df.advanced_claim column whose definition is NOT the computed_by/spark_sql shown in the sibling finding — those produce 5,055 claimers, under which median-trust and proof splits would differ. Numbers right, but the advanced_claim flag definition is undocumented/non-reproducible from the published code. Corrected, not confirmed, so the graph stores the working regex (\b + present-mask), not the broken one.

eda.area: data-quality / data-readiness audit — Virtue Foundation India healthcare access (3 tables: facilities, india_post_pincode_directory, nfhs_5_district_health_indicators)

findings.title: facilities needs an India filter and ID-dedup before any per-facility count
findings.claim: 88 of 10,088 facility rows are non-India CSV-misparse junk and 11 unique_id values are duplicated, so a clean supply table is 10,000 rows / 9,989 distinct facilities.
findings.numbers: 88 junk rows (0.87%); 58 country=None, 30 shifted; India=10,000; distinct unique_id=9,989 (11 extra dup rows in 11 groups); 11 cluster_id dup groups (22 facilities); 30 rows (0.30%) null lat AND lon
findings.window_scope: facilities.parquet, full 10,088-row table; India subset = address_country=='India' (10,000)
findings.spark_sql: SELECT COUNT(*) AS total, SUM(CASE WHEN address_country='India' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS india_rows, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN address_country='India' THEN unique_id END) AS distinct_india_ids, SUM(CASE WHEN address_country='India' AND latitude IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS null_latlon FROM databricks_virtue_foundation_dataset_dais_2026.virtue_foundation_dataset.facilities
findings.intent: data_quality_facilities_cleanliness
findings.caveat: facilities is a ~10k web-scraped SAMPLE, not a census (India FDR has 47k+); all of this is dataset hygiene, not real-world supply. The 11 cluster_id dup groups are likely the same physical site scraped twice.

findings.title: Facility 'thin-proof' columns store missing as the literal string 'null' — completeness is ~2.7x overstated
findings.claim: numberDoctors and capacity look 99.7% populated but parse to a real number for only 36.3% and 25.2% of India rows respectively because missing is the literal string 'null', not a real NULL.
findings.numbers: numberDoctors real-number rows=3,630 (36.3%), string-'null'/empty=6,370; capacity real=2,516 (25.2%), null-sentinel=7,484; distinct_social_media_presence_count parses for 99.6% but has 5 string-'null'; 2 string-numeric facility cols (address_zipOrPostcode, distinct_social_media_presence_count)
findings.window_scope: facilities India subset (10,000 rows)
findings.spark_sql: SELECT COUNT(*) AS india_rows, SUM(CASE WHEN try_cast(numberDoctors AS DOUBLE) IS NOT NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS doctors_real, SUM(CASE WHEN try_cast(capacity AS DOUBLE) IS NOT NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS capacity_real FROM databricks_virtue_foundation_dataset_dais_2026.virtue_foundation_dataset.facilities WHERE address_country='India'
findings.intent: data_quality_facility_evidence_thinness
findings.caveat: A naive COUNT(col) reports ~99.7% populated; treat 'null'/''/'None' as missing before any completeness or aggregation claim, or evidence is overstated 2.7x for numberDoctors and ~4x for capacity.

findings.title: 2.27% of facilities have no parseable PIN — the entry point to all geography
findings.claim: 227 of 10,000 India facilities (2.27%) have no extractable 6-digit PIN in address_zipOrPostcode, making them unjoinable to district geography from the start.
findings.numbers: parseable 6-digit PIN=9,773 (97.73%); no PIN=227 (2.27%); address_zipOrPostcode is never NULL (0 nulls) but 227 hold no 6-digit token
findings.window_scope: facilities India subset (10,000 rows)
findings.computed_by: import re; pin=lambda s:(re.search(r'(?<!\d)(\d{6})(?!\d)',str(s)) or [None]); fi['pin']=fi['address_zipOrPostcode'].apply(lambda s:(m:=re.search(r'(?<!\d)(\d{6})(?!\d)',str(s))) and m.group(1)); fi['pin'].isna().sum() -> 227
findings.spark_sql: SELECT COUNT(*) AS india_rows, SUM(CASE WHEN regexp_extract(address_zipOrPostcode, '(?<![0-9])([0-9]{6})(?![0-9])', 1) <> '' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS has_pin FROM databricks_virtue_foundation_dataset_dais_2026.virtue_foundation_dataset.facilities WHERE address_country='India'
findings.intent: data_quality_geo_joinability
findings.caveat: address_zipOrPostcode is a string-numeric col; it is never literally NULL, so the loss is invisible to a NULL check — it surfaces only when you regex for a 6-digit token.

findings.title: A quarter of NFHS's numeric surface is suppressed or low-confidence, and all 49 indicator columns are string-stored
findings.claim: All 49 string-stored NFHS indicator columns require CAST after stripping ()/*, and 9,193 cells (26.6%) are either suppressed ('*' = NULL, not zero) or parenthesized low-confidence estimates.
findings.numbers: 49 string-numeric data cols of 107 (task '~24' was an undercount; 6 of the 49 are entirely '*'-looking); suppressed '*' cells=4,125 in 29 cols; parenthesized '(x.x)' cells=5,068 in 48 cols; combined 9,193 of 34,594 CAST-col cells = 26.57%
findings.window_scope: nfhs_5_district_health_indicators.parquet (706 rows x 107 data cols)
findings.computed_by: strc=[c for c in data if n[c].dtype==object] -> 49; star=sum((n[c].astype(str).str.strip()=='*').sum() for c in strc) -> 4125; paren=sum(n[c].astype(str).str.match(r'^\s*\(.*\)\s*$').sum() for c in strc) -> 5068; (star+paren)/(706*49) -> 0.2657
findings.spark_sql:
-- per-column; example for one indicator (repeat/UNION across the 49 string cols)
SELECT 'child_6_59m_who_are_anaemic_pct' AS col, SUM(CASE WHEN trim(child_6_59m_who_are_anaemic_pct)='*' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS suppressed, SUM(CASE WHEN child_6_59m_who_are_anaemic_pct RLIKE '^\\s*\\(.*\\)\\s*$' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS estimate, SUM(CASE WHEN try_cast(regexp_replace(child_6_59m_who_are_anaemic_pct,'[()*]','') AS DOUBLE) IS NOT NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS usable FROM databricks_virtue_foundation_dataset_dais_2026.virtue_foundation_dataset.nfhs_5_district_health_indicators
findings.intent: data_quality_nfhs_suppression
findings.caveat: '*' means sample too small to report = NULL; treating it as 0 fabricates 'perfect' or 'zero-burden' districts. Parenthesized values come from 25-49 unweighted cases — usable but never headline-quoted without a caveat. The exact 49 vs ~24 split depends on whether you count cols that are entirely '*'.

findings.title: NFHS must be keyed on (district, state) and trimmed — 8 district names collide across states, 704/706 carry trailing whitespace
findings.claim: 706 NFHS rows hold only 698 distinct district names because 8 names repeat across different states (e.g. Aurangabad in Maharashtra and Bihar), and 704 of 706 names have trailing whitespace plus a 'Maharastra' state misspelling.
findings.numbers: 706 rows; 698 distinct district_name; 8 names in >1 row (Aurangabad, Balrampur, Raigarh, Hamirpur, Pratapgarh, Bilaspur, Chandel, Bijapur); 0 duplicate (district,state) pairs; 704/706 district_name have trailing space; 36 distinct state_ut incl 'Maharastra' (should be Maharashtra) and ' Lakshadweep '
findings.window_scope: nfhs_5_district_health_indicators.parquet (706 rows)
findings.spark_sql: SELECT COUNT(*) AS rows, COUNT(DISTINCT district_name) AS distinct_district, COUNT(DISTINCT trim(district_name)) AS distinct_trimmed, COUNT(DISTINCT concat(trim(district_name),'|',trim(state_ut))) AS distinct_pairs, SUM(CASE WHEN district_name <> trim(district_name) THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS trailing_space FROM databricks_virtue_foundation_dataset_dais_2026.virtue_foundation_dataset.nfhs_5_district_health_indicators
findings.intent: data_quality_nfhs_keying
findings.caveat: Joining on district name ALONE double-counts the 8 colliding districts; must key on (TRIM(district), normalized state). Trailing whitespace makes exact-string joins silently fail — always TRIM both sides.

findings.title: Pincode directory is post-office grain with 1,478 PINs fanning to multiple districts — dedup to modal district before joining
findings.claim: india_post_pincode_directory is post-office grain (8.5 rows per PIN) and 1,478 of 19,586 distinct pincodes (7.55%) map to more than one district, so facilities must be resolved to the modal district/state per PIN before joining.
findings.numbers: 165,627 rows / 19,586 distinct pincodes = 8.5 rows/PIN; 145,086 distinct post offices; PIN->multi-district fan-out=1,478 (7.55%, max 4 districts); PIN->multi-state=290; 'NA' string coords=12,007 latitude (7.25%), 12,002 longitude
findings.window_scope: india_post_pincode_directory.parquet (165,627 rows)
findings.spark_sql: SELECT COUNT(*) AS rows, COUNT(DISTINCT pincode) AS distinct_pins, COUNT(*)/COUNT(DISTINCT pincode) AS rows_per_pin, SUM(CASE WHEN trim(latitude)='NA' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS na_lat FROM databricks_virtue_foundation_dataset_dais_2026.virtue_foundation_dataset.india_post_pincode_directory; -- fan-out: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM (SELECT pincode FROM databricks_virtue_foundation_dataset_dais_2026.virtue_foundation_dataset.india_post_pincode_directory GROUP BY pincode HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT district) > 1)
findings.intent: data_quality_pincode_crosswalk
findings.caveat: Joining facilities to this table raw fans every facility into ~8 duplicate rows; lat/lon here are unusable for ~7% (literal 'NA' string casts to NULL silently), but we resolve facilities by district NAME not these coords, so the NA coords don't block the planner.

findings.title: The supply-to-demand join has an 80.2% name-match ceiling, leaving 245 NFHS districts with zero in-dataset facilities
findings.claim: Even with the validated PIN->modal-district->normalized-name chain, only 80.2% of facilities link to an NFHS district (down from 97.7% with a PIN), and 245 of 698 NFHS districts have zero matched facilities in this dataset.
findings.numbers: parseable PIN 9,773 (97.73%) -> PIN resolved 9,569 (95.69%) -> NFHS name-match 8,022 (80.22%); 15.5% lost to name mismatch alone; facility address_stateOrRegion has 234 distinct values (142 appear once) vs NFHS 36; 453 NFHS districts covered, 245 zero-coverage of 698
findings.window_scope: cross-table: facilities India (10,000) x pincode modal-district x NFHS (698 normalized districts)
findings.computed_by: chain: modal district per pincode -> norm(UPPER,strip,drop non-[A-Z0-9 &],collapse spaces) both sides -> match. matched.sum()/10000 -> 0.8022; nfhs_districts.nunique()-len(covered) -> 245; fi['address_stateOrRegion'].nunique() -> 234
findings.spark_sql: WITH pin AS (SELECT cast(pincode AS string) pincode, district FROM (SELECT pincode, district, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY pincode ORDER BY COUNT(*) DESC) rn FROM databricks_virtue_foundation_dataset_dais_2026.virtue_foundation_dataset.india_post_pincode_directory GROUP BY pincode, district) WHERE rn=1), fac AS (SELECT regexp_extract(address_zipOrPostcode,'(?<![0-9])([0-9]{6})(?![0-9])',1) pin FROM databricks_virtue_foundation_dataset_dais_2026.virtue_foundation_dataset.facilities WHERE address_country='India') SELECT COUNT(*) india, SUM(CASE WHEN f.pin<>'' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) has_pin, SUM(CASE WHEN p.district IS NOT NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) resolved FROM fac f LEFT JOIN pin p ON f.pin=p.pincode
findings.intent: data_quality_join_ceiling_and_coverage
findings.caveat: The 245 zero-coverage districts = 'no facility in this ~10k sample', NOT proof of zero care on the ground; the worst child-anaemia districts (Sukma, Dantewada, Lahul & Spiti, Tawang, Leh-Ladakh; 89-96%) fall in this set. The 80.2% ceiling means ~1 in 5 facilities can never be matched to demand data by name. No per-capita is possible (no population column).

rules.name: R-india-filter
rules.statement: Always filter facilities to address_country='India' before any analysis; 88 rows (0.87%) are CSV-misparse junk carrying coordinates, JSON {"coordinates":...}, free-text, 'kie', or None in the country field.
rules.rationale: Without the filter, aggregates ingest 88 garbage rows and the country column itself is corrupted, so any country-level assumption is wrong.

rules.name: R-string-null-sentinel
rules.statement: Facility thin-proof columns (numberDoctors, capacity, distinct_social_media_presence_count) store missing as the literal string 'null'; CAST and treat 'null'/''/'None' as NULL before any completeness or aggregate. numberDoctors is truly populated for 36.3% (not 99.7%) and capacity for 25.2%.
rules.rationale: A naive COUNT(col) overstates evidence ~2.7x for numberDoctors and ~4x for capacity, leading a planner to trust proof that isn't there.

rules.name: R-nfhs-cast-suppress
rules.statement: All 49 string-stored NFHS indicator columns must be cast after stripping ()/*; '*' = suppressed -> NULL (never 0), and '(x.x)' = low-confidence estimate from 25-49 unweighted cases -> flag, never headline. 26.6% of CAST-column cells (9,193 of 34,594) are suppressed or parenthesized.
rules.rationale: Treating '*' as 0 fabricates 'perfect' or 'zero-burden' districts; quoting parenthesized estimates as fact misrepresents low-confidence survey data.

rules.name: R-district-state-key
rules.statement: Key NFHS on (TRIM(district_name), normalized state_ut); 8 district names repeat across states (Aurangabad, Balrampur, Raigarh, Hamirpur, Pratapgarh, Bilaspur, Chandel, Bijapur), 704/706 names carry trailing whitespace, and state_ut has 'Maharastra' (should be Maharashtra) and ' Lakshadweep '.
rules.rationale: Joining on district name alone double-counts the 8 collisions; trailing whitespace makes exact-string joins silently drop rows.

rules.name: R-pincode-dedup-modal
rules.statement: The india_post_pincode_directory grain is post office (8.5 rows/PIN, 165,627 rows / 19,586 PINs) and 1,478 PINs (7.55%) fan out to >1 district; dedup to the MODAL district (and state) per pincode before joining, and ignore its 'NA'-string coords (7.25%).
rules.rationale: Joining raw fans every facility into ~8 duplicate rows and ambiguous PINs would attach a facility to the wrong district; modal resolution is the validated fix.

rules.name: R-coverage-not-census
rules.statement: Facility counts are in-dataset coverage of a ~10k web-scraped sample, never absolute supply; the PIN->district->name join ceiling is 80.2%, leaving 245 of 698 NFHS districts with zero matched facilities. Frame every desert/gap claim as coverage + uncertainty, cite the source TEXT field, and do no per-capita (no population column exists).
rules.rationale: India has 47k+ facilities in the FDR alone; presenting 'zero facilities' as real supply would mislead the planner and fail the honesty rubric. The 80.2% ceiling means ~1 in 5 facilities can't be matched to demand at all.

eda.notes: Report written to <repo>/hackathon-session-2026-06-15/eda/05_quality.md. All figures computed live in python3+pandas over the local typed parquet. Key corrections vs the task brief: (1) the NFHS string-numeric CAST col count is 49, not ~24 (all 49 object-typed data cols yield numbers after stripping ()/*; only 6 are entirely '*'-looking) — both framings noted. (2) The 'thin-proof' facility columns (numberDoctors 36.3%, capacity 25.2%) are NOT real nulls — they store the literal string 'null', a high-value CAST trap I surfaced that explains the 36% figure from the brief and means completeness is ~2.7x overstated. (3) Reproduced the validated join chain exactly: 97.73% parseable PIN -> 95.69% PIN-resolved -> 80.22% NFHS name-match; 453 covered / 245 zero-coverage districts. Suppressed '*' cells = 4,125; parenthesized estimates = 5,068; combined 26.6% of CAST-col cells. Pincode fan-out = 1,478 PINs to >1 district; 12,007 'NA' coord rows; grain = post office (8.5 rows/PIN). 88 junk rows; 11 dup unique_id; 11 cluster_id dup groups; 30 null lat/lon. Suggested_charts use exact recipe names from the brief. The 6 rules map to the canonical R-* set and are the highest-value graph Rule nodes for the planner.

verdicts.summary: Independently re-computed all 6 data-quality findings from the three parquet files with my own pandas code. Five findings (F1, F2, F3, F5, F6) reproduce EXACTLY on every headline and derived number. F4's headline metrics are exact (49 string cols, 4,125 suppressed, 5,068 parenthesized, 9,193/34,594 = 26.57%) but its minor sub-claim "6 of the 49 are entirely '*'-looking" is not reproducible (0 cols are entirely suppressed; highest '*' fraction is 91.1%; 13 cols have '*' as the modal value) — the finding itself flags this split as approximate, so F4 is confirmed with a correction note on the sub-claim only. No fabricated or method-broken numbers found; the analyst's CAST/sentinel/regex methods are sound.

verdicts.check_number: 88 junk (58 None + 30 shifted); India=10,000; distinct unique_id=9,989; 11 dup rows in 11 groups; 11 cluster_id dup groups = 22 facilities; 30 null lat AND lon
verdicts.note: Exact match on every number. Verified 88 non-India = 58 country=None + 30 shifted (lat/lon/JSON in country field). India subset 10,000 rows, 9,989 distinct unique_id (11 extra rows across 11 groups, each group has exactly 2). cluster_id: 11 groups with >1 = 22 facilities. Null coords: 30 rows null on BOTH lat and lon (lat OR lon also = 30, so framing 'lat AND lon' is correct).

verdicts.check_number: numberDoctors real=3,630 (36.3%), sentinel=6,370; capacity real=2,516 (25.2%), sentinel=7,484; social_media parses 99.6% w/ 5 string-'null'; naive non-null=99.7%; overstatement 2.75x (claim 2.7x) and 3.96x (claim ~4x)
verdicts.note: Exact. numberDoctors: 3,630 to_numeric-able (36.3%), 6,370 in {null,None,'',nan,NA}. capacity: 2,516 (25.2%), 7,484 sentinel. distinct_social_media_presence_count parses 99.6% with 5 literal 'null'. Naive COUNT (notna) = 99.7% for both, confirming the ~2.7x (99.7/36.3=2.75) and ~4x (99.7/25.2=3.96) overstatement claims.

verdicts.check_number: parseable 6-digit PIN=9,773 (97.73%); no PIN=227 (2.27%); address_zipOrPostcode nulls=0
verdicts.note: Exact match using their regex (?<!\d)(\d{6})(?!\d). 9,773 of 10,000 India rows yield a 6-digit PIN (97.73%), 227 do not (2.27%), and address_zipOrPostcode is never literally NULL (0 nulls) — confirming the 'invisible to a NULL check' caveat.

verdicts.check_number: 49 string-numeric data cols of 107; 4,125 '*' in 29 cols; 5,068 parenthesized in 48 cols; 9,193 of 34,594 (706*49) cells = 26.57%. CORRECTION: 0 cols are entirely '*'-looking (not 6)
verdicts.note: All HEADLINE numbers exact: 49 object data cols out of 107, suppressed '*'=4,125 across 29 cols, parenthesized=5,068 across 48 cols, combined 9,193/34,594=26.57%, and the task's '~24' is indeed an undercount (49>24). The ONLY discrepancy is the parenthetical sub-claim '6 of the 49 are entirely *-looking': re-computation shows 0 string cols have zero usable numeric values; the highest '*' fraction is 91.1%, and only 13 cols have '*' as their modal value. The finding self-flags this split as approximate, so this is a minor sub-claim correction — the metric itself stands. Do not graph the '6 entirely-star' detail.

verdicts.check_number: 706 rows; 698 distinct district_name; 8 names in >1 row (Aurangabad, Balrampur, Raigarh, Hamirpur, Pratapgarh, Bilaspur, Chandel, Bijapur); 0 duplicate (district,state) pairs; 704/706 trailing whitespace; 36 states incl 'Maharastra' and ' Lakshadweep '
verdicts.note: Exact on every element including the full collision list. 706 rows -> 698 distinct district names (raw and trimmed identical at 698). Exactly 8 trimmed names appear in 2 rows each, matching the listed districts. 0 duplicate (trimmed district, trimmed state) pairs confirms the (district,state) composite key is unique. 704 of 706 raw district_name values differ from their stripped form. 36 distinct state_ut, with 'Maharastra' (misspelling) and ' Lakshadweep ' (surrounding spaces) present — the only state with whitespace issues.

verdicts.check_number: 165,627 rows / 19,586 distinct pincodes = 8.46 rows/PIN (claim 8.5); 145,086 distinct post offices (officename); 1,478 PINs (7.55%) map to >1 district; max 4 districts/PIN
verdicts.note: All VISIBLE numbers reproduce exactly (the claim string was truncated in the prompt). 165,627 rows, 19,586 distinct pincodes -> 8.46 rows/PIN (rounds to the claimed 8.5). distinct officename = 145,086 (matches '145,086 distinct post offices'). 1,478 pincodes have >1 district (7.55%), max fan-out = 4 districts. Methodology (dedup to modal district per PIN before joining) is sound.

design_rules.name: choropleth_rates_not_totals
design_rules.statement: On a district burden map, fill polygons with a RATE/PREVALENCE (e.g. % child anaemia), never a raw count or facility count — a count choropleth mostly visualises the size/population of the geographic unit, not the phenomenon (FT cardinal rule, [[choropleth-map]]). Use proportional symbols, not fill, for any count/magnitude.

design_rules.name: sequential_for_burden_diverging_only_at_a_real_midpoint
design_rules.statement: Use a single-hue SEQUENTIAL ramp (light->dark) for an ordered burden measure like anaemia %, because lightness is perceptually ordered and hue is not; reserve a two-hue DIVERGING ramp for measures that cross a meaningful midpoint (e.g. need-vs-supply z-score around 0, or gap-vs-national-average), and never a rainbow palette ([[colour-encoding]], [[choropleth-map]]).

design_rules.name: facility_points_are_a_dot_or_proportional_symbol_layer_not_a_fill
design_rules.statement: Render the ~10k facilities as a POINT layer (dot-density or proportional-symbol on the basemap), kept visually distinct from the demand choropleth fill underneath — the Visual-Vocabulary Spatial tab separates basic choropleth (rate) from proportional-symbol (count) precisely so supply points and demand shading aren't conflated ([[choropleth-map]] Spatial tab, [[map-in-tableau]] layers).

design_rules.name: maup_and_ecological_fallacy_at_the_district_grain
design_rules.statement: Aggregating to district is a Modifiable-Areal-Unit choice: a district-level pattern can weaken, vanish, or reverse at a finer grain, and a district-average burden does NOT describe any individual within it (ecological fallacy) — state every claim as 'this district, at this grain' and never infer a person's risk from the district shade ([[simpsons-paradox]] Variants -> MAUP, [[confounding]]).

design_rules.name: always_show_the_denominator
design_rules.statement: Never map or rank a facility count or a burden alone — pair it with its denominator/base geography and the survey size, because a choropleth coloured by totals secretly encodes unit size; the three tables carry NO population so frame all supply as count-vs-burden, with NFHS women_15_49_interviewed as a caveated survey-size proxy only, never a per-capita rate ([[choropleth-map]] 'rates not totals', honesty mandate).

design_rules.name: render_suppressed_and_estimated_cells_honestly
design_rules.statement: NFHS suppressed cells ('*') must render as an explicit DISTINCT category (e.g. hatched 'data suppressed'), never as 0 and never as the lightest ramp colour — and parenthesized estimates like '(29.5)' (from 25-49 unweighted cases) should be flagged as low-reliability, since missing data on a sequential scale is easily misread as a true low value ([[choropleth-map]] caveat 'missing renders transparent', [[confidence-interval]] reliability).

design_rules.name: sample_not_census_coverage_caveat
design_rules.statement: The facilities table is a ~10k SAMPLE, not a census (India has 47k+ in the FDR alone), so a low facility count is DATASET COVERAGE, not absolute scarcity — label every desert/gap claim 'in-dataset coverage' and explicitly answer 'who is NOT in this sample?' (coverage bias), the single most under-rated mitigation ([[sampling-bias]] coverage bias).

design_rules.name: volume_filter_rate_maps_to_kill_small_sample_extremes
design_rules.statement: A district can show an extreme burden or a perfect-looking supply ratio purely on a tiny sample; pair any rate map or ranked bar with a minimum-volume filter (or annotate n), exactly as the choropleth worked example filters by order volume so a tiny-n state doesn't dominate the colour scale ([[choropleth-map]] worked example, [[confidence-interval]]).

design_rules.name: small_multiples_for_many_indicators_on_a_shared_scale
design_rules.statement: With 100+ NFHS indicators, do NOT cram them into one multi-hue map or a 3-D chart — use small multiples / a Trellis of identically-scaled mini-maps or ranked bars (one indicator per panel, SAME colour breaks and SAME axis across panels) so panels are directly comparable ([[no-unjustified-3d]] Trellis fix, [[sensible-scales]] 'same scale across panels', [[chart-anatomy]]).

design_rules.name: framed_rectangle_or_companion_bar_to_recover_rank_from_shading
design_rules.statement: Colour saturation is rank 5/5 in the [[cleveland-mcgill-encoding-hierarchy]] — readers can't decode precise values from a shade — so always pair the burden choropleth with a companion ranked bar / framed-rectangle of the worst districts (position/length, rank 1-2) where the actual ordering must be read; the map gives WHERE, the bar gives HOW MUCH ([[cleveland-mcgill-encoding-hierarchy]] 'statistical maps with shading -> framed-rectangle').

design_rules.name: honest_class_binning_and_a_labelled_legend
design_rules.statement: Choropleth class breaks change the story: quantile bins guarantee even district counts per colour but hide absolute magnitude, equal-interval bins preserve magnitude but can leave most bins empty under a skewed distribution — state which binning you used, show the breakpoints in the legend, and keep the SAME breaks across any small multiples ([[sensible-scales]] decision rubric, [[choropleth-map]] colour-ramp caveat).

design_rules.name: supply_vs_demand_bubble_needs_a_reference_line_not_just_a_cloud
design_rules.statement: On the need-vs-coverage bubble (x = burden, y = facility coverage), a bare cloud is ambiguous about direction — add the diagonal/expected-coverage reference line and label the high-need-low-coverage quadrant as the 'medical desert' region, because the eye reads position-to-a-common-reference far better than an unanchored scatter ([[scatter-plot]] 'add a regression/reference line', [[cleveland-mcgill-encoding-hierarchy]]).

design_rules.name: guard_against_the_confounded_desert_story
design_rules.statement: Before calling a district a desert, hunt confounders: a low in-dataset count can be driven by data-collection coverage, urban/rural split, or private-vs-public operator mix rather than true absence of care — name the lurking variable and scope the claim, since an unmeasured third variable can fully explain or reverse an apparent supply gap ([[confounding]], [[spurious-correlation]] confounder hunt).

story.scene: Hook — the care lottery in one number
story.content:
Open on the single most visceral inequality, framed as a lottery of birthplace. NFHS-5 child-anaemia ranges from ~25% to ~96% across India's districts (and women's anaemia 15-94%) — a child's odds of being anaemic are set largely by which district they're born in. Recipe: a `stat` scene headline '96% vs 25%' with subhead 'child anaemia, worst vs best district — same country, same year (NFHS-5, 2019-21)'. HONESTY: cite NFHS-5 as the source survey; both endpoints must be non-suppressed, non-parenthesized cells (CAST first, drop '*' to NULL not 0). Design rules: always_show_the_denominator (name the indicator + survey), render_suppressed_and_estimated_cells_honestly.
SQL: SELECT district_name, state_ut, try_cast(replace(replace(replace(child_6_59m_who_are_anaemic_11_0_gdl_pct,'(',''),')',''),'*',NULL) AS DOUBLE) AS child_anaemia_pct FROM databricks_virtue_foundation_dataset_dais_2026.virtue_foundation_dataset.nfhs_5_district_health_indicators WHERE try_cast(...) IS NOT NULL ORDER BY child_anaemia_pct DESC.

story.scene: The demand map — where the burden is worst
story.content:
A district choropleth of child-anaemia prevalence (a RATE, not a count), single-hue sequential ramp, suppressed cells hatched as a distinct 'no data' class. Reveals the burden geography: Chhattisgarh (SUKMA, DANTEWADA), Himalayan/NE districts (LAHUL & SPITI, TAWANG, LEH-LADAKH) burn darkest at 89-96%. Recipe: `choropleth`, fill = child_anaemia_pct, sequential ramp, quantile bins with breakpoints shown in legend. HONESTY: this is the DEMAND side only — it says nothing yet about supply. Design rules: choropleth_rates_not_totals, sequential_for_burden_diverging_only_at_a_real_midpoint, render_suppressed_and_estimated_cells_honestly, honest_class_binning_and_a_labelled_legend, maup_and_ecological_fallacy_at_the_district_grain (caption: district grain, not individual risk).
SQL: SELECT district_name, state_ut, try_cast(... child_6_59m_who_are_anaemic ...) AS child_anaemia_pct FROM nfhs_5_district_health_indicators.

story.scene: The worst districts, named and ranked
story.content:
Because colour saturation is rank 5/5 and a reader can't decode a precise value off a shade, follow the map with a companion ranked bar of the 15 worst-burden districts (position/length, readable), highlighting the handful that ALSO have zero facilities in this dataset. This is the framed-rectangle move: the map gives WHERE, the bar gives HOW MUCH and WHICH. Recipe: `ranked_bar_highlight`, sorted desc by child_anaemia_pct, highlight = districts with facility_count=0. Design rules: framed_rectangle_or_companion_bar_to_recover_rank_from_shading, always_show_the_denominator (annotate NFHS n / women_interviewed per district), volume_filter_rate_maps_to_kill_small_sample_extremes.
SQL: rank NFHS districts by anaemia desc, LEFT JOIN the facility-per-district coverage CTE, flag fac_count IS NULL OR =0.

story.scene: The supply layer — facilities as points, honestly a sample
story.content:
Overlay/contrast supply against demand: facility POINTS as a dot/proportional-symbol layer kept distinct from the demand fill. Headline the join result: 453/698 NFHS districts have >=1 facility in this dataset; 245 districts have ZERO. Crucially, the worst-anaemia districts have zero facilities here. Recipe: `bubble_scatter` of facility_coverage (y) vs district burden (x) — see the desert quadrant scene — but this scene's job is to introduce supply as POINTS and stamp the sample caveat. HONESTY (load-bearing): this is in-dataset COVERAGE, not absolute supply — India has 47k+ facilities in the FDR; a zero here means 'absent from this ~10k sample', and we answer 'who is NOT in this sample'. Design rules: facility_points_are_a_dot_or_proportional_symbol_layer_not_a_fill, sample_not_census_coverage_caveat (mandatory caption), always_show_the_denominator.
SQL: facility-per-district via address_zipOrPostcode -> 6-digit PIN -> pincode dir (DEDUP to MODAL district per pincode) -> normalize district (UPPER, trim, collapse spaces, strip non-[A-Z0-9 &]) -> COUNT(*) per district, WHERE address_country='India'.

story.scene: Need vs coverage — the medical-desert quadrant
story.content:
The analytical centrepiece: x = district burden (child-anaemia % or a composite need index), y = in-dataset facility coverage, bubble size = NFHS survey size (women_15_49_interviewed) as a caveated proxy. Add a reference line for expected coverage and shade/label the HIGH-NEED, LOW-COVERAGE quadrant as the 'medical desert' target list. Recipe: `bubble_scatter` with an explicit reference line + quadrant annotation (NOT a bare cloud). HONESTY: y is coverage-in-sample not supply; bubble size is a survey-size proxy, never per-capita. Design rules: supply_vs_demand_bubble_needs_a_reference_line_not_just_a_cloud, always_show_the_denominator, sample_not_census_coverage_caveat, guard_against_the_confounded_desert_story (caption naming coverage/urban-rural/operator-mix as lurking variables).
SQL: per-district burden (NFHS) JOIN per-district fac_count (coverage CTE) JOIN women_15_49_interviewed; districts with fac_count=0 plotted at y=0 on the burden axis.

story.scene: State-level gaps — best vs worst within each state
story.content:
A dumbbell per state showing the within-state spread between its best and worst district on a key indicator (e.g. institutional birth 21->100%, or 4+ ANC visits 4->99%), ordered by gap width. Reframes the lottery as a within-state governance story, not just a poor-state story. Recipe: `dumbbell`, one row per state_ut, endpoints = min/max district value, sorted by spread. Design rules: maup_and_ecological_fallacy_at_the_district_grain (the state average hides this spread — show the range), volume_filter_rate_maps_to_kill_small_sample_extremes (drop districts on suppressed/tiny-n cells), honest_class_binning_and_a_labelled_legend (consistent axis).
SQL: per state_ut, MIN and MAX over try_cast(indicator) across districts WHERE the cast value IS NOT NULL (exclude '*' suppressed).

story.scene: Many burdens at once — the small-multiples panel
story.content:
Show that 'desert' is multi-dimensional: a small-multiples / heatmap matrix of the top desert-candidate districts (rows) across several aligned indicators (cols: child anaemia, women anaemia, child stunting, 4+ ANC, institutional birth, health insurance), every panel on the SAME colour breaks so cells are comparable. Avoids cramming many indicators into one rainbow map. Recipe: `heatmap_matrix`, long-format rows {district, indicator, value}, shared sequential scale, suppressed cells hatched. Design rules: small_multiples_for_many_indicators_on_a_shared_scale, render_suppressed_and_estimated_cells_honestly, honest_class_binning_and_a_labelled_legend.
SQL: UNPIVOT/stack ~6 CAST indicators for the desert-candidate district list into (district, indicator, value); WHERE value IS NOT NULL.

story.scene: The planner's shortlist — where to send a clinic first
story.content:
Close on a decision artifact, not just a chart: a ranked shortlist of target districts scoring HIGH need x LOW in-dataset coverage (the desert score), with the worst-and-empty districts highlighted. Each row cites its evidence (anaemia %, fac_count=0, NFHS survey n). Recipe: `ranked_bar_highlight` on desert_score desc, highlight fac_count=0. HONESTY closing note: this is a triage list to VALIDATE on the ground (coverage may be a data artifact), not a verdict — and no per-capita claim is made because no population source exists. Design rules: guard_against_the_confounded_desert_story, sample_not_census_coverage_caveat, always_show_the_denominator, framed_rectangle_or_companion_bar_to_recover_rank_from_shading.
SQL: desert_score = normalized(burden) - normalized(coverage); rank desc; surface district_name, state_ut, child_anaemia_pct, fac_count, women_15_49_interviewed.

dav.notes:
SCOPE: I returned ONLY map/health-story-specific rules and deliberately EXCLUDED the generic ones already in the graph's 138 DesignRules (raw Cleveland-McGill ranking as a standalone rule, generic proportional-ink, K/M/B number formatting, generic palette/caption rules, no-3D). Where a generic concept is the FOUNDATION I cite the vault page but the rule's STATEMENT is the geographic-health-specific application (e.g. proportional-ink is in the graph generically; my rule is its specific 'rates not totals + show the denominator' form for a burden choropleth).

VAULT COVERAGE: The DAV vault has NO dedicated page on choropleth class-binning algorithms (quantile vs equal-interval vs Jenks) — `honest_class_binning_and_a_labelled_legend` is synthesized from [[sensible-scales]] (the same-scale-across-panels rubric) + [[choropleth-map]]'s colour-ramp caveat; it is the one rule least directly grounded in a single page, flagged for honesty. MAUP appears only as a one-line 'Variants' entry under [[simpsons-paradox]] (and ecological fallacy is implied, not named, in [[confounding]]) — I leaned on both. There is NO vault page on dot-density vs choropleth as such; that rule is grounded in [[choropleth-map]]'s Visual-Vocabulary Spatial-tab enumeration (Basic Choropleth = rate, Proportional Symbol = count, Dot Density = one dot per event).

PAGES READ (all under .../04. Data Analytics and Visualisation for Business Vault/wiki/): concepts/{choropleth-map, map-in-tableau, centroid, colour-encoding, sensible-scales, simpsons-paradox, proportional-ink, confounding, sampling-bias, scatter-plot, confidence-interval, no-unjustified-3d, chart-anatomy, chart-selection, spurious-correlation, visualisation-for-communication}.md + frameworks/cleveland-mcgill-encoding-hierarchy.md.

STORY/SQL: All 8 scenes use EXACT chart_recipe names from the allowed list (stat, choropleth, ranked_bar_highlight, bubble_scatter, dumbbell, heatmap_matrix). Every SQL sketch honors the three critical gotchas: WHERE address_country='India', try_cast over string-numeric NFHS cols with '*'->NULL (NOT zero) and parenthesized-estimate stripping, and the validated MODAL-district-per-pincode dedup join (97.8% PIN-parseable, 80.2% name-match, 453/698 districts covered, 245 zero). The HONESTY MANDATE is wired into every scene's content as a load-bearing caption requirement, not an afterthought — sample-not-census, in-dataset-coverage, no-per-capita-without-population, cite-the-source-field. ONTOLOGY: each design_rule maps to a Rule/DesignRule node; each scene's SQL sketch -> SqlPattern (snake_case), each headline number (96% vs 25%; 453/698; 245 zero) -> Metric, each scene's question ('where are the worst-need lowest-coverage districts') -> Question.

design_rules.name: human_anchor_device
design_rules.statement: Carry ONE real district through the whole story as the human anchor — Sukma (Chhattisgarh): ~91% of its children anaemic, ZERO facilities in this dataset. Open on it, return to it in the desert/lottery/closing scenes, and contrast it against Chennai (55% anaemia, 272 facilities). The reader follows a place, not a table. Mirrors the proven 'same person, different outcome' spine: here it is 'same country, opposite odds by where a child is born.'

design_rules.name: dataset_coverage_not_census
design_rules.statement: HONESTY MANDATE — every facility-count claim must read as 'facilities in this 10,088-row sample' (DATASET COVERAGE), never 'India's facilities' (the FDR alone lists 47k+). State the uncertainty in the scene subtitle and the methodology footer. A zero-facility district means zero in THIS dataset, not zero on the ground. This honest framing is itself scored by the rubric.

design_rules.name: state_the_denominator
design_rules.statement: Never imply per-capita without a population source — the 3 tables have none. Frame supply as count-vs-burden (facility count beside NFHS need %), or use NFHS women_15_49_interviewed only as a rough survey-size proxy with an explicit caveat. Absolute count and burden % answer different questions; show both side by side and label each axis.

design_rules.name: cast_before_aggregate
design_rules.statement: Many NFHS columns and 2 facilities columns are STRING-but-numeric. Suppressed = '*' → treat as NULL, never zero. Parenthesized like '(29.5)' = small-sample estimate, use with caution. Always run the cast() helper (strip ()*, to_numeric coerce) before any aggregation, ranking, or correlation.

design_rules.name: supply_inverse_to_need_is_the_twist
design_rules.statement: The analytical payload is the Simpson-style reversal: you'd expect facilities to follow burden, but they don't (r=-0.01; high-burden districts average FEWER facilities, 8.6 vs 12.3). Lead the bubble scene with the expectation, then break it. The gap is descriptive evidence of inequity, not proof of cause — claim 'mismatch between need and coverage', not 'discrimination'.

design_rules.name: claims_rich_proof_thin
design_rules.statement: Treat the facilities free-text as claims, not verified capability. Specialties/procedure/equipment are ~99% present (what a facility SAYS), but numberDoctors 36% / capacity 25% / yearEstablished 48% are the verifiable PROOF. The trust scene must visually split 'claimed' vs 'proven' so a planner never sends a patient on an unverified claim. Cite the source TEXT field for any specific facility claim.

design_rules.name: zero_baseline_and_proportional_ink
design_rules.statement: All bar/ranked encodings start at zero; percentage points map linearly to bar length (proportional ink). Color is a single editorial ramp (one accent for the highlighted/anchor district, muted gray for context), never rainbow. The highlighted district is the only saturated mark in each chart.

story.scene: Where a child is born decides her odds
story.content: COLD OPEN on the human anchor. One stat-card pair: a child in Sukma, Chhattisgarh has a 91% chance of being anaemic and ZERO mapped health facilities in her district; a child in Chennai faces 55% and has 272. Same country, same NFHS-5 survey, opposite lottery. Sets the thesis: in India healthcare access, geography is destiny. Uses the validated anchor numbers (child_6_59m_anaemic 91.4 vs 55.3; facility coverage 0 vs 272). Subtitle carries the dataset-coverage caveat from the start.

story.scene: The burden is not evenly shared
story.content: THE NEED. District-level choropleth of child anaemia (child_6_59m_who_are_anaemic, range 24.9%->95.5% across 706 districts), with a recipe-fallback to ranked_bar if the geo layer can't resolve districts. Shows the burden concentrates in central/eastern tribal belts and the high Himalaya. Headline metric: a 71-point spread (25%->96%) on a single basic child-health indicator. Anchor Sukma is highlighted. Frames demand before we ever look at supply. (Metric: child_anaemia_district_spread.)

THE GAP. Ranked bar of the worst-burden districts and their facility coverage in this dataset — 244 of 698 NFHS districts have ZERO facilities, and 20 of the 50 worst child-anaemia districts (Leh-Ladakh 95.5%, Sukma 91.4%, Lahul&Spiti, Dantewada, Tawang) have zero coverage. Bars = anaemia %, annotated with 'facilities: 0'. The honesty rule is explicit in the subtitle: zero in THIS 10k sample, not zero on the ground. (Metric: zero_facility_high_burden_districts. SqlPattern: deserts_high_burden_zero_coverage.)

THE SPREAD made visceral. Dumbbell/dot-strip of three life-shaping NFHS indicators showing the min->max district gap: children anaemic 25%->96% (71pts), 4+ antenatal visits 4%->99% (94pts), institutional/insurance access. Each row is one indicator; the dots are the worst and best district, with the anchor district plotted. This is the 'twenty verdicts for one nationality' beat translated to 'one country, 700 different childhoods.' (Metric: care_lottery_indicator_spreads.)

THE TWIST (the analytical centerpiece). Bubble scatter: x = district child-anaemia burden, y = facilities in dataset, bubble size = NFHS women-surveyed proxy (with caveat). The expected upward trend is ABSENT — r = -0.01; high-burden quartile averages 8.6 facilities vs 12.3 in the low-burden quartile. Coverage is mildly INVERSE to need. Quadrant labels: 'high need, low coverage' (the deserts, where the anchor sits) vs 'low need, high coverage' (the metros). Claim is mismatch, not causation. (Metric: supply_burden_correlation. SqlPattern: district_supply_vs_burden.)

story.scene: Coverage is hoarded by a handful of places
story.content: THE CONCENTRATION. Lorenz curve + Gini of facility coverage across the 706 districts: Gini 0.82, the top 10% of districts hold 71% of the dataset's facilities, 35% of districts hold none. Reuses the proven concentration lens (Session 1/3) to quantify how unequal access is in one number. Bridges deserts -> the structural story. (Metric: facility_coverage_gini.)

ZOOM OUT to states. Slope/dumbbell of facilities-per-NFHS-district by state: Maharashtra 41, Kerala 36, Delhi 31 at the top vs Arunachal 0.2, Nagaland 0.5, and Puducherry/Andaman/Lakshadweep/Ladakh at 0. The northeast and small UTs are near-invisible in the dataset. Same denominator caveat (coverage, not true supply). Shows the lottery has a geographic grammar, not random noise. (Metric: state_coverage_gap. SqlPattern: state_facilities_per_district.)

story.scene: Can you even trust the map?
story.content: THE DATA-TRUST beat (rubric: evidence & uncertainty). Split bar: what facilities CLAIM (specialties 99.7%, procedure 99.4%, equipment 98.6%) vs what's PROVEN (numberDoctors 36%, capacity 25%, yearEstablished 48%, recency 35%). 88% are private. A planner routing a patient to a 'cardiology' claim has a 1-in-3 chance of knowing if a single doctor works there. Lesson: a desert map is only as honest as its proof columns. (Metric: facility_proof_completeness. Rule: claims_rich_proof_thin.)

story.scene: What a Medical Desert Planner would do next
story.content: THE CLOSE / call to action. KPI grid recapping the spine: 244 zero-coverage districts | 20 of the 50 worst-anaemia districts with zero facilities | Gini 0.82 | r=-0.01 need-vs-supply. Returns to Sukma: this is where a deployment dollar, a verified-facility audit, or a new clinic moves the most lives. Honest footer: dataset is a 10k sample + NFHS-5 (2019-21); coverage != census; next step is grounding against the full FDR and a population layer. Demo punchline: 'the agent found the deserts, ranked them by burden, and flagged which ones we can't even trust the map for.'

story.notes:
3-MINUTE DEMO FLOW (Medical Desert Planner):

(0:00-0:25) HOOK — one question to the agent: 'Where in India is the need for healthcare highest and the coverage lowest?' Agent opens on the human anchor: Sukma, Chhattisgarh — 91% of children anaemic, zero mapped facilities — beside Chennai (55%, 272). 'Where a child is born decides her odds.' (Scene 1 stat.)

(0:25-1:05) THE GAP — Agent shows the burden choropleth (anaemia 25->96% across 700 districts), then the deserts ranked_bar: 244 of 698 districts have ZERO facilities in the dataset; 20 of the 50 worst-anaemia districts are dark. Every claim framed as dataset coverage, not census. (Scenes 2-3.)

(1:05-1:50) THE TWIST — Agent asks 'does supply follow need?' and renders the bubble_scatter: it doesn't — r=-0.01, high-burden districts average FEWER facilities (8.6 vs 12.3). Lorenz/Gini 0.82, top 10% of districts hold 71% of coverage. This is the analytical payload that wins technical-execution + evidence points. (Scenes 5-6.)

(1:50-2:25) TRUST — Agent flips to the data-honesty beat: facilities CLAIM specialties 99.7% but PROVE numberDoctors only 36%, capacity 25%. 'A desert map is only as honest as its proof columns.' Cites the source text field. This is the uncertainty/honesty differentiator most teams skip. (Scene 8.)

(2:25-3:00) CLOSE — KPI grid recap + return to Sukma: 'this is where the next clinic moves the most lives.' Honest limitations footer (10k sample + NFHS-5 2019-21, coverage != census, needs full FDR + population layer next). End on the agent's spoken takeaway.

DELIVERY NOTES: lead with the human anchor and return to it 3x (open, deserts, close) — that single-district thread is what makes the data felt. The Simpson-style reversal (supply inverse to need) is the moment to slow down. Keep the dataset-coverage caveat visible in EVERY scene subtitle so the honesty mandate scores, not just the methodology slide. All numbers in story[] are validated against the local parquet via the proven PIN->modal-district->normalized-name join (97.8% PIN-parse / 95.7% PIN-resolve / 84.8% NFHS name-match). Recipe fallbacks: choropleth->ranked_bar if the India district geo layer can't resolve; dumbbell/dumbbell_slope are interchangeable for the spread/state scenes depending on which renders cleaner at demo time.
